---
name: generic-design-system
description: Complete design system reference for any project - colors, typography, spacing, components, animations. Adapts to project theme and tech stack. Use when implementing UI, choosing colors, creating animations, or ensuring brand consistency.
---

# Generic Design System

Comprehensive design system reference that adapts to any project's visual language.

## Color System

### Defining Project Colors

Every project should define:
- **Primary**: Main brand color
- **Secondary**: Supporting color
- **Accent**: Highlight/CTA color
- **Background**: Base canvas color
- **Foreground**: Primary text color
- **Muted**: Secondary elements
- **Destructive**: Error/danger states
- **Success**: Positive states
- **Warning**: Caution states

### Color Mode Support

| Mode | Considerations |
|------|----------------|
| Light | Higher contrast, softer backgrounds |
| Dark | Reduced blue light, darker backgrounds |
| High Contrast | WCAG AAA compliance |

### Contrast Requirements

| Use Case | Minimum Ratio |
|----------|---------------|
| Body text | 4.5:1 (AA) |
| Large text (24px+) | 3:1 (AA) |
| UI components | 3:1 (AA) |
| Enhanced | 7:1 (AAA) |

## Typography

### Font Stack

```css
/* System fonts for performance */
--font-sans: -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, 'Segoe UI', Roboto, sans-serif;
--font-mono: 'SF Mono', Monaco, 'Courier New', monospace;

/* Custom fonts loaded via @font-face or Google Fonts */
```

### Type Scale

| Name | Size | Line Height | Use |
|------|------|-------------|-----|
| xs | 12px | 16px | Captions, labels |
| sm | 14px | 20px | Secondary text |
| base | 16px | 24px | Body text |
| lg | 18px | 28px | Lead paragraphs |
| xl | 20px | 28px | Section headers |
| 2xl | 24px | 32px | Page headers |
| 3xl | 30px | 36px | Hero text |
| 4xl | 36px | 40px | Display |

### Font Weights

| Weight | Value | Use |
|--------|-------|-----|
| Normal | 400 | Body text |
| Medium | 500 | UI elements |
| Semibold | 600 | Subheadings |
| Bold | 700 | Headings |

## Spacing System

### Base Unit

Recommend 4px or 8px base unit for consistency.

| Token | 4px Base | 8px Base | Use |
|-------|----------|----------|-----|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | None |
| 1 | 4px | 8px | Tight spacing |
| 2 | 8px | 16px | Default gap |
| 3 | 12px | 24px | Section padding |
| 4 | 16px | 32px | Card padding |
| 5 | 20px | 40px | Section margins |
| 6 | 24px | 48px | Page margins |
| 8 | 32px | 64px | Large sections |

### Layout Grid

```css
/* Common container widths */
--container-sm: 640px;
--container-md: 768px;
--container-lg: 1024px;
--container-xl: 1280px;
--container-2xl: 1536px;
```

## Border Radius

| Token | Value | Use |
|-------|-------|-----|
| none | 0 | Sharp corners |
| sm | 2px | Subtle rounding |
| DEFAULT | 4px | Buttons, inputs |
| md | 6px | Cards |
| lg | 8px | Modals |
| xl | 12px | Large cards |
| 2xl | 16px | Hero sections |
| full | 9999px | Pills, avatars |

## Shadows

| Token | Use |
|-------|-----|
| sm | Subtle elevation (buttons) |
| DEFAULT | Standard elevation (cards) |
| md | Medium elevation (dropdowns) |
| lg | High elevation (modals) |
| xl | Maximum elevation (popovers) |

## Animation

### Timing Functions

```css
--ease-default: cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 0.2, 1);
--ease-in: cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 1, 1);
--ease-out: cubic-bezier(0, 0, 0.2, 1);
--ease-in-out: cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 0.2, 1);
--ease-bounce: cubic-bezier(0.68, -0.55, 0.265, 1.55);
```

### Duration

| Token | Duration | Use |
|-------|----------|-----|
| fast | 100ms | Micro-interactions |
| DEFAULT | 200ms | Most transitions |
| slow | 300ms | Complex animations |
| slower | 500ms | Page transitions |

### GPU-Accelerated Properties

**DO animate:**
- `transform` (translate, scale, rotate)
- `opacity`

**AVOID animating:**
- `width`, `height`
- `top`, `left`, `right`, `bottom`
- `margin`, `padding`
- `background-color` (OK for simple)

## Components

### Buttons

| Variant | Use |
|---------|-----|
| Primary | Main actions (Submit, Save) |
| Secondary | Alternative actions |
| Ghost | Subtle actions |
| Outline | Less prominent actions |
| Destructive | Delete, remove actions |

### Form Inputs

- Clear focus states
- Error states with messages
- Placeholder text as hint
- Label always visible
- Required indicator

### Cards

- Consistent padding
- Optional header/footer
- Hover state for interactive
- Focus state for keyboard

### Modals/Dialogs

- Focus trapped inside
- Escape to close
- Click outside to close (optional)
- Centered or slide-in
- Dark overlay

## Icons

### Size Tokens

| Token | Size | Use |
|-------|------|-----|
| xs | 12px | Inline with small text |
| sm | 16px | Inline with body |
| md | 20px | Buttons, inputs |
| lg | 24px | Headers |
| xl | 32px | Features |

### Stroke Width

- 1.5px for light icons
- 2px for standard icons
- 2.5px for bold icons

## Responsive Breakpoints

| Token | Min Width | Devices |
|-------|-----------|---------|
| sm | 640px | Large phones |
| md | 768px | Tablets |
| lg | 1024px | Laptops |
| xl | 1280px | Desktops |
| 2xl | 1536px | Large desktops |

## Dark Mode Strategy

```css
/* CSS Variables approach */
:root {
  --background: #ffffff;
  --foreground: #000000;
}

[data-theme="dark"] {
  --background: #000000;
  --foreground: #ffffff;
}

/* Or media query */
@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) {
  :root {
    --background: #000000;
    --foreground: #ffffff;
  }
}
```

## Implementation Patterns

### CSS Variables (Recommended)

```css
:root {
  --color-primary: #3b82f6;
  --spacing-4: 16px;
  --radius-md: 6px;
}

.button {
  background: var(--color-primary);
  padding: var(--spacing-4);
  border-radius: var(--radius-md);
}
```

### Tailwind Config

```js
module.exports = {
  theme: {
    extend: {
      colors: { primary: '#3b82f6' },
      spacing: { '4': '16px' },
      borderRadius: { md: '6px' }
    }
  }
}
```

## See Also

- Project-specific design system skills for detailed brand requirements
- `.claude/workflow-checklist.md` - Styling checklist
- Project's `CLAUDE.md` for design constraints

name: generic-feature-developer
description: Guide feature development with architecture patterns for any tech stack. Covers frontend, backend, full-stack, and automation projects. Use when adding new features, modifying systems, or planning changes.
---

# Generic Feature Developer

Guide feature development across any tech stack with proven architecture patterns.

## Feature Development Workflow

### 1. Understand Requirements
- Read existing CLAUDE.md and documentation
- Identify affected systems/files
- List dependencies and constraints
- Define acceptance criteria

### 2. Plan Architecture
- Choose appropriate patterns
- Design data flow
- Identify edge cases
- Plan error handling

### 3. Implement Incrementally
- Small, testable changes
- Commit frequently
- Update task file as you go
- Get approval at milestones

### 4. Test & Document
- Write/update tests
- Update documentation
- Run full test suite
- Performance check

## Architecture Patterns by Project Type

### Static Sites (HTML/CSS/JS)

```
project/
├── index.html          # Main entry
├── css/
│   ├── reset.css       # CSS reset
│   ├── variables.css   # CSS custom properties
│   └── style.css       # Main styles
├── js/
│   ├── main.js         # Entry point
│   └── utils.js        # Utilities
└── assets/
    ├── images/
    └── fonts/
```

**Patterns:**
- CSS custom properties for theming
- ES modules for organization
- Event delegation for dynamic content
- Progressive enhancement

### React/Next.js Applications

```
src/
├── components/
│   ├── ui/             # Base UI components
│   ├── features/       # Feature-specific
│   └── layout/         # Layout components
├── hooks/              # Custom React hooks
├── lib/                # Utilities, helpers
├── stores/             # State management
├── services/           # API calls
└── types/              # TypeScript types
```

**Patterns:**
- Container/Presentational split
- Custom hooks for logic reuse
- Zustand/Redux for global state
- React Query for server state
- Compound components for complex UI

### Node.js/Express Backend

```
src/
├── routes/             # Route handlers
├── controllers/        # Business logic
├── services/           # External services
├── models/             # Data models
├── middleware/         # Express middleware
├── utils/              # Helpers
└── config/             # Configuration
```

**Patterns:**
- MVC or service layer
- Dependency injection
- Repository pattern for data
- Error middleware
- Validation middleware

### NestJS Backend

```
src/
├── modules/
│   └── [feature]/
│       ├── feature.module.ts
│       ├── feature.controller.ts
│       ├── feature.service.ts
│       └── dto/
├── common/
│   ├── guards/
│   ├── decorators/
│   └── interceptors/
└── prisma/             # Database
```

**Patterns:**
- Module-based organization
- DTOs for validation
- Guards for auth
- Interceptors for transforms
- Prisma for type-safe DB

### Python Automation

```
automation/
├── scripts/
│   └── main.py         # Entry point
├── lib/
│   ├── validators.py   # Data validation
│   └── utils.py        # Utilities
├── data/
│   └── config.json     # Configuration
├── tests/
│   └── test_main.py
└── .github/
    └── workflows/
        └── schedule.yml
```

**Patterns:**
- Dry run mode for testing
- Environment-based config
- Structured logging
- Error handling with retry
- GitHub Actions for scheduling

## Common Feature Types

### Adding a New UI Component

1. Create component file
2. Add to exports
3. Create stories/tests
4. Use in feature
5. Document usage

### Adding an API Endpoint

1. Define route
2. Create controller/handler
3. Add validation
4. Implement service logic
5. Add tests
6. Document endpoint

### Adding State Management

1. Define state shape
2. Create store/slice
3. Add actions/mutations
4. Connect to components
5. Persist if needed

### Adding Authentication

1. Choose strategy (JWT, sessions, OAuth)
2. Implement auth service
3. Add middleware/guards
4. Protect routes
5. Handle tokens
6. Add login/logout UI

### Adding Database Feature

1. Design schema
2. Create migration
3. Update models/types
4. Add repository/service
5. Integrate with API
6. Test with fixtures

## Error Handling Patterns

### Frontend

```typescript
try {
  const result = await apiCall();
  // Handle success
} catch (error) {
  if (error instanceof NetworkError) {
    showRetryDialog();
  } else if (error instanceof ValidationError) {
    showFieldErrors(error.fields);
  } else {
    showGenericError();
    reportToSentry(error);
  }
}
```

### Backend

```typescript
// Express error middleware
app.use((err, req, res, next) => {
  logger.error(err);

  if (err instanceof ValidationError) {
    return res.status(400).json({ errors: err.errors });
  }
  if (err instanceof AuthError) {
    return res.status(401).json({ message: 'Unauthorized' });
  }

  res.status(500).json({ message: 'Internal server error' });
});
```

## Data Flow Patterns

### Unidirectional (React)

```
User Action → Dispatch → Store → State Change → Re-render
```

### Request/Response (API)

```
Client Request → Middleware → Controller → Service → Database
                                                       ↓
Client Response ← Serializer ← Controller ← Service ←
```

### Event-Driven

```
Event Emitted → Event Bus → Subscribers → Side Effects
```

## Integration Patterns

### Third-Party APIs

1. Create service wrapper
2. Handle authentication
3. Implement retry logic
4. Cache responses
5. Handle rate limits
6. Mock for tests

### Database Operations

1. Use transactions for multi-step
2. Handle constraints/conflicts
3. Implement soft delete
4. Add audit logging
5. Optimize queries

## Testing Strategy

| Layer | Test Type | Tools |
|-------|-----------|-------|
| UI | Component | Testing Library |
| Logic | Unit | Jest, Vitest |
| API | Integration | Supertest |
| E2E | End-to-end | Playwright, Cypress |
| Visual | Snapshot | Storybook, Percy |

## Performance Considerations

### Frontend
- Code splitting
- Lazy loading
- Memoization
- Virtual scrolling
- Debounce/throttle

### Backend
- Database indexing
- Query optimization
- Caching (Redis)
- Connection pooling
- Background jobs

## See Also

- Project-specific feature developer skills
- `CLAUDE.md` - Project workflow rules
- `.claude/automation-guide.md` - Development workflow

name: generic-ux-designer
description: Professional UX expertise for any project type. Covers user research, design thinking, interaction patterns, accessibility, and design critique. Use when designing new features, improving UX, conducting design reviews, or evaluating user flows.
---

# Generic UX Designer

Professional UX expertise that adapts to any project's constraints and user needs.

## Design Thinking Process

### 1. Empathize
- Understand user needs
- Identify pain points
- Map user journeys
- Research competitors

### 2. Define
- Problem statements
- User personas
- Success metrics
- Constraints

### 3. Ideate
- Brainstorm solutions
- Explore alternatives
- Consider edge cases
- Sketch concepts

### 4. Prototype
- Low-fidelity first
- Test assumptions
- Iterate quickly
- Validate with users

### 5. Test
- Usability testing
- A/B testing
- Analytics review
- Iterate based on data

## User Psychology Principles

### Cognitive Load
- Reduce choices (Hick's Law)
- Chunk information (Miller's Law)
- Use progressive disclosure
- Minimize user memory load

### Visual Hierarchy
- Size indicates importance
- Contrast draws attention
- Whitespace groups elements
- F-pattern/Z-pattern reading

### Behavioral Patterns
- Users scan, not read
- Familiarity breeds trust
- Immediate feedback essential
- Error prevention > recovery

### Emotional Design
- Delight in micro-interactions
- Personality through copy
- Trust through transparency
- Satisfaction through completion

## Interaction Patterns

### Navigation

| Pattern | Use Case |
|---------|----------|
| Top nav | Simple sites, < 7 items |
| Side nav | Complex apps, many sections |
| Bottom nav | Mobile apps, < 5 items |
| Hamburger | Mobile, space-constrained |
| Tabs | Related content sections |
| Breadcrumbs | Deep hierarchies |

### Forms

| Pattern | Benefit |
|---------|---------|
| Single column | Faster completion |
| Inline validation | Immediate feedback |
| Field grouping | Logical flow |
| Smart defaults | Reduced effort |
| Progress indicator | Clear expectations |
| Error summary | Easy correction |

### Data Display

| Pattern | Use Case |
|---------|----------|
| Cards | Browsable content |
| Tables | Comparable data |
| Lists | Sequential items |
| Timeline | Chronological events |
| Dashboard | At-a-glance metrics |
| Search + filters | Large datasets |

### Feedback

| Pattern | Use Case |
|---------|----------|
| Toast | Non-blocking info |
| Modal | Blocking/important |
| Inline | Contextual feedback |
| Banner | Global notifications |
| Progress | Long operations |
| Skeleton | Loading states |

## Mobile UX Considerations

### Touch Targets
- Minimum 44x44px
- 8px spacing between targets
- Thumb-friendly placement
- Bottom sheet for actions

### Gestures
- Tap: Primary action
- Swipe: Navigate/reveal
- Long press: Secondary actions
- Pinch: Zoom (media)

### Mobile Patterns
- Bottom navigation
- Pull to refresh
- Infinite scroll (carefully)
- Full-screen modals
- Native keyboard handling

## Accessibility (UX Perspective)

### Inclusive Design
- Design for extremes, works for all
- Consider temporary disabilities
- Support different input methods
- Test with real users

### Key Considerations

| Aspect | UX Approach |
|--------|-------------|
| Vision | High contrast, scalable text, alt text |
| Motor | Large targets, keyboard nav, no timing |
| Cognitive | Simple language, clear structure |
| Hearing | Captions, visual indicators |

### Testing
- Screen reader walkthrough
- Keyboard-only navigation
- Color blindness simulation
- Reduced motion mode

## Design Critique Framework

### Criteria

| Aspect | Questions |
|--------|-----------|
| Usability | Can users complete tasks? |
| Clarity | Is purpose immediately clear? |
| Consistency | Follows established patterns? |
| Hierarchy | Most important stands out? |
| Feedback | Users know system state? |
| Error handling | Recoverable mistakes? |
| Accessibility | Works for everyone? |

### Constructive Feedback

**Instead of:** "This is confusing"
**Say:** "Users might not understand X because Y. Consider Z."

**Instead of:** "I don't like this"
**Say:** "This pattern may cause issues because... Alternative approach..."

## Common UX Patterns

### Onboarding
1. Progressive disclosure
2. Contextual tips
3. Interactive tutorials
4. Skip option always
5. Revisitable help

### Empty States
1. Explain what goes here
2. Guide to first action
3. Show example content
4. Make it delightful

### Loading States
1. Skeleton screens > spinners
2. Progress indicators for long ops
3. Optimistic UI when safe
4. Background loading when possible

### Error States
1. Explain what happened
2. Suggest how to fix
3. Provide recovery action
4. Don't blame user

### Success States
1. Confirm action completed
2. Show next steps
3. Celebrate achievements
4. Enable sharing if appropriate

## Content Strategy

### Microcopy
- Be concise
- Use active voice
- Avoid jargon
- Be human
- Guide action

### Labels & Instructions
- Clear and specific
- Explain why (if needed)
- Example formats
- Required vs optional

### Error Messages
- Specific problem
- How to fix
- No technical jargon
- Apologetic when appropriate

## Metrics That Matter

### Engagement
- Time on task
- Completion rate
- Return visits
- Feature adoption

### Satisfaction
- NPS score
- CSAT surveys
- Support tickets
- User feedback

### Performance
- Task success rate
- Error rate
- Time to first action
- Abandonment points

## Design Review Checklist

### Functionality
- [ ] Primary task achievable
- [ ] Edge cases handled
- [ ] Error states designed
- [ ] Loading states designed

### Usability
- [ ] Clear visual hierarchy
- [ ] Obvious next actions
- [ ] Consistent with patterns
- [ ] Minimal cognitive load

### Accessibility
- [ ] Keyboard navigable
- [ ] Screen reader tested
- [ ] Color contrast sufficient
- [ ] Touch targets sized

### Polish
- [ ] Micro-interactions smooth
- [ ] Copy clear and helpful
- [ ] Responsive breakpoints
- [ ] Performance acceptable

## See Also

- Project-specific UX skills for detailed requirements
- `.claude/skills/generic-design-system/` - Visual design
- `CLAUDE.md` - Project constraints

name: generic-design-system
description: Complete design system reference for any project - colors, typography, spacing, components, animations. Adapts to project theme and tech stack. Use when implementing UI, choosing colors, creating animations, or ensuring brand consistency.
---

# Generic Design System

Comprehensive design system reference that adapts to any project's visual language.

## Color System

### Defining Project Colors

Every project should define:
- **Primary**: Main brand color
- **Secondary**: Supporting color
- **Accent**: Highlight/CTA color
- **Background**: Base canvas color
- **Foreground**: Primary text color
- **Muted**: Secondary elements
- **Destructive**: Error/danger states
- **Success**: Positive states
- **Warning**: Caution states

### Color Mode Support

| Mode | Considerations |
|------|----------------|
| Light | Higher contrast, softer backgrounds |
| Dark | Reduced blue light, darker backgrounds |
| High Contrast | WCAG AAA compliance |

### Contrast Requirements

| Use Case | Minimum Ratio |
|----------|---------------|
| Body text | 4.5:1 (AA) |
| Large text (24px+) | 3:1 (AA) |
| UI components | 3:1 (AA) |
| Enhanced | 7:1 (AAA) |

## Typography

### Font Stack

```css
/* System fonts for performance */
--font-sans: -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, 'Segoe UI', Roboto, sans-serif;
--font-mono: 'SF Mono', Monaco, 'Courier New', monospace;

/* Custom fonts loaded via @font-face or Google Fonts */
```

### Type Scale

| Name | Size | Line Height | Use |
|------|------|-------------|-----|
| xs | 12px | 16px | Captions, labels |
| sm | 14px | 20px | Secondary text |
| base | 16px | 24px | Body text |
| lg | 18px | 28px | Lead paragraphs |
| xl | 20px | 28px | Section headers |
| 2xl | 24px | 32px | Page headers |
| 3xl | 30px | 36px | Hero text |
| 4xl | 36px | 40px | Display |

### Font Weights

| Weight | Value | Use |
|--------|-------|-----|
| Normal | 400 | Body text |
| Medium | 500 | UI elements |
| Semibold | 600 | Subheadings |
| Bold | 700 | Headings |

## Spacing System

### Base Unit

Recommend 4px or 8px base unit for consistency.

| Token | 4px Base | 8px Base | Use |
|-------|----------|----------|-----|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | None |
| 1 | 4px | 8px | Tight spacing |
| 2 | 8px | 16px | Default gap |
| 3 | 12px | 24px | Section padding |
| 4 | 16px | 32px | Card padding |
| 5 | 20px | 40px | Section margins |
| 6 | 24px | 48px | Page margins |
| 8 | 32px | 64px | Large sections |

### Layout Grid

```css
/* Common container widths */
--container-sm: 640px;
--container-md: 768px;
--container-lg: 1024px;
--container-xl: 1280px;
--container-2xl: 1536px;
```

## Border Radius

| Token | Value | Use |
|-------|-------|-----|
| none | 0 | Sharp corners |
| sm | 2px | Subtle rounding |
| DEFAULT | 4px | Buttons, inputs |
| md | 6px | Cards |
| lg | 8px | Modals |
| xl | 12px | Large cards |
| 2xl | 16px | Hero sections |
| full | 9999px | Pills, avatars |

## Shadows

| Token | Use |
|-------|-----|
| sm | Subtle elevation (buttons) |
| DEFAULT | Standard elevation (cards) |
| md | Medium elevation (dropdowns) |
| lg | High elevation (modals) |
| xl | Maximum elevation (popovers) |

## Animation

### Timing Functions

```css
--ease-default: cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 0.2, 1);
--ease-in: cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 1, 1);
--ease-out: cubic-bezier(0, 0, 0.2, 1);
--ease-in-out: cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 0.2, 1);
--ease-bounce: cubic-bezier(0.68, -0.55, 0.265, 1.55);
```

### Duration

| Token | Duration | Use |
|-------|----------|-----|
| fast | 100ms | Micro-interactions |
| DEFAULT | 200ms | Most transitions |
| slow | 300ms | Complex animations |
| slower | 500ms | Page transitions |

### GPU-Accelerated Properties

**DO animate:**
- `transform` (translate, scale, rotate)
- `opacity`

**AVOID animating:**
- `width`, `height`
- `top`, `left`, `right`, `bottom`
- `margin`, `padding`
- `background-color` (OK for simple)

## Components

### Buttons

| Variant | Use |
|---------|-----|
| Primary | Main actions (Submit, Save) |
| Secondary | Alternative actions |
| Ghost | Subtle actions |
| Outline | Less prominent actions |
| Destructive | Delete, remove actions |

### Form Inputs

- Clear focus states
- Error states with messages
- Placeholder text as hint
- Label always visible
- Required indicator

### Cards

- Consistent padding
- Optional header/footer
- Hover state for interactive
- Focus state for keyboard

### Modals/Dialogs

- Focus trapped inside
- Escape to close
- Click outside to close (optional)
- Centered or slide-in
- Dark overlay

## Icons

### Size Tokens

| Token | Size | Use |
|-------|------|-----|
| xs | 12px | Inline with small text |
| sm | 16px | Inline with body |
| md | 20px | Buttons, inputs |
| lg | 24px | Headers |
| xl | 32px | Features |

### Stroke Width

- 1.5px for light icons
- 2px for standard icons
- 2.5px for bold icons

## Responsive Breakpoints

| Token | Min Width | Devices |
|-------|-----------|---------|
| sm | 640px | Large phones |
| md | 768px | Tablets |
| lg | 1024px | Laptops |
| xl | 1280px | Desktops |
| 2xl | 1536px | Large desktops |

## Dark Mode Strategy

```css
/* CSS Variables approach */
:root {
  --background: #ffffff;
  --foreground: #000000;
}

[data-theme="dark"] {
  --background: #000000;
  --foreground: #ffffff;
}

/* Or media query */
@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) {
  :root {
    --background: #000000;
    --foreground: #ffffff;
  }
}
```

## Implementation Patterns

### CSS Variables (Recommended)

```css
:root {
  --color-primary: #3b82f6;
  --spacing-4: 16px;
  --radius-md: 6px;
}

.button {
  background: var(--color-primary);
  padding: var(--spacing-4);
  border-radius: var(--radius-md);
}
```

### Tailwind Config

```js
module.exports = {
  theme: {
    extend: {
      colors: { primary: '#3b82f6' },
      spacing: { '4': '16px' },
      borderRadius: { md: '6px' }
    }
  }
}
```

- Project-specific design system skills for detailed brand requirements

### Adding a New UI Component

1. Create component file
2. Add to exports
3. Create stories/tests
4. Use in feature
5. Document usage

### Adding an API Endpoint

1. Define route
2. Create controller/handler
3. Add validation
4. Implement service logic
5. Add tests
6. Document endpoint

### Adding State Management

1. Define state shape
2. Create store/slice
3. Add actions/mutations
4. Connect to components
5. Persist if needed

### Adding Authentication

1. Choose strategy (JWT, sessions, OAuth)
2. Implement auth service
3. Add middleware/guards
4. Protect routes
5. Handle tokens
6. Add login/logout UI

### Adding Database Feature

1. Design schema
2. Create migration
3. Update models/types
4. Add repository/service
5. Integrate with API
6. Test with fixtures

## Error Handling Patterns

### Frontend

```typescript
try {
  const result = await apiCall();
  // Handle success
} catch (error) {
  if (error instanceof NetworkError) {
    showRetryDialog();
  } else if (error instanceof ValidationError) {
    showFieldErrors(error.fields);
  } else {
    showGenericError();
    reportToSentry(error);
  }
}
```

### Backend

```typescript
// Express error middleware
app.use((err, req, res, next) => {
  logger.error(err);

  if (err instanceof ValidationError) {
    return res.status(400).json({ errors: err.errors });
  }
  if (err instanceof AuthError) {
    return res.status(401).json({ message: 'Unauthorized' });
  }

  res.status(500).json({ message: 'Internal server error' });
});
```

## Data Flow Patterns

### Unidirectional (React)

```
User Action → Dispatch → Store → State Change → Re-render
```

### Request/Response (API)

```
Client Request → Middleware → Controller → Service → Database
                                                       ↓
Client Response ← Serializer ← Controller ← Service ←
```

### Event-Driven

```
Event Emitted → Event Bus → Subscribers → Side Effects
```

## Integration Patterns

### Third-Party APIs

1. Create service wrapper
2. Handle authentication
3. Implement retry logic
4. Cache responses
5. Handle rate limits
6. Mock for tests

### Database Operations

1. Use transactions for multi-step
2. Handle constraints/conflicts
3. Implement soft delete
4. Add audit logging
5. Optimize queries

## Testing Strategy

| Layer | Test Type | Tools |
|-------|-----------|-------|
| UI | Component | Testing Library |
| Logic | Unit | Jest, Vitest |
| API | Integration | Supertest |
| E2E | End-to-end | Playwright, Cypress |
| Visual | Snapshot | Storybook, Percy |

## Performance Considerations

### Frontend
- Code splitting
- Lazy loading
- Memoization
- Virtual scrolling
- Debounce/throttle

### Backend
- Database indexing
- Query optimization
- Caching (Redis)
- Connection pooling
- Background jobs

## See Also

- Project-specific feature developer skills
- `CLAUDE.md` - Project workflow rules
- `.claude/automation-guide.md` - Development workflow

---
name: generic-ux-designer
description: Professional UX expertise for any project type. Covers user research, design thinking, interaction patterns, accessibility, and design critique. Use when designing new features, improving UX, conducting design reviews, or evaluating user flows.
---

# Generic UX Designer

Professional UX expertise that adapts to any project's constraints and user needs.

## Design Thinking Process

### 1. Empathize
- Understand user needs
- Identify pain points
- Map user journeys
- Research competitors

### 2. Define
- Problem statements
- User personas
- Success metrics
- Constraints

### 3. Ideate
- Brainstorm solutions
- Explore alternatives
- Consider edge cases
- Sketch concepts

### 4. Prototype
- Low-fidelity first
- Test assumptions
- Iterate quickly
- Validate with users

### 5. Test
- Usability testing
- A/B testing
- Analytics review
- Iterate based on data

## User Psychology Principles

### Cognitive Load
- Reduce choices (Hick's Law)
- Chunk information (Miller's Law)
- Use progressive disclosure
- Minimize user memory load

### Visual Hierarchy
- Size indicates importance
- Contrast draws attention
- Whitespace groups elements
- F-pattern/Z-pattern reading

### Behavioral Patterns
- Users scan, not read
- Familiarity breeds trust
- Immediate feedback essential
- Error prevention > recovery

### Emotional Design
- Delight in micro-interactions
- Personality through copy
- Trust through transparency
- Satisfaction through completion

## Interaction Patterns

### Navigation

| Pattern | Use Case |
|---------|----------|
| Top nav | Simple sites, < 7 items |
| Side nav | Complex apps, many sections |
| Bottom nav | Mobile apps, < 5 items |
| Hamburger | Mobile, space-constrained |
| Tabs | Related content sections |
| Breadcrumbs | Deep hierarchies |

### Forms

| Pattern | Benefit |
|---------|---------|
| Single column | Faster completion |
| Inline validation | Immediate feedback |
| Field grouping | Logical flow |
| Smart defaults | Reduced effort |
| Progress indicator | Clear expectations |
| Error summary | Easy correction |

### Data Display

| Pattern | Use Case |
|---------|----------|
| Cards | Browsable content |
| Tables | Comparable data |
| Lists | Sequential items |
| Timeline | Chronological events |
| Dashboard | At-a-glance metrics |
| Search + filters | Large datasets |

### Feedback

| Pattern | Use Case |
|---------|----------|
| Toast | Non-blocking info |
| Modal | Blocking/important |
| Inline | Contextual feedback |
| Banner | Global notifications |
| Progress | Long operations |
| Skeleton | Loading states |

## Mobile UX Considerations

### Touch Targets
- Minimum 44x44px
- 8px spacing between targets
- Thumb-friendly placement
- Bottom sheet for actions

### Gestures
- Tap: Primary action
- Swipe: Navigate/reveal
- Long press: Secondary actions
- Pinch: Zoom (media)

### Mobile Patterns
- Bottom navigation
- Pull to refresh
- Infinite scroll (carefully)
- Full-screen modals
- Native keyboard handling

## Accessibility (UX Perspective)

### Inclusive Design
- Design for extremes, works for all
- Consider temporary disabilities
- Support different input methods
- Test with real users

### Key Considerations

| Aspect | UX Approach |
|--------|-------------|
| Vision | High contrast, scalable text, alt text |
| Motor | Large targets, keyboard nav, no timing |
| Cognitive | Simple language, clear structure |
| Hearing | Captions, visual indicators |

### Testing
- Screen reader walkthrough
- Keyboard-only navigation
- Color blindness simulation
- Reduced motion mode

## Design Critique Framework

### Criteria

| Aspect | Questions |
|--------|-----------|
| Usability | Can users complete tasks? |
| Clarity | Is purpose immediately clear? |
| Consistency | Follows established patterns? |
| Hierarchy | Most important stands out? |
| Feedback | Users know system state? |
| Error handling | Recoverable mistakes? |
| Accessibility | Works for everyone? |

### Constructive Feedback

**Instead of:** "This is confusing"
**Say:** "Users might not understand X because Y. Consider Z."

**Instead of:** "I don't like this"
**Say:** "This pattern may cause issues because... Alternative approach..."

## Common UX Patterns

### Onboarding
1. Progressive disclosure
2. Contextual tips
3. Interactive tutorials
4. Skip option always
5. Revisitable help

### Empty States
1. Explain what goes here
2. Guide to first action
3. Show example content
4. Make it delightful

### Loading States
1. Skeleton screens > spinners
2. Progress indicators for long ops
3. Optimistic UI when safe
4. Background loading when possible

### Error States
1. Explain what happened
2. Suggest how to fix
3. Provide recovery action
4. Don't blame user

### Success States
1. Confirm action completed
2. Show next steps
3. Celebrate achievements
4. Enable sharing if appropriate

## Content Strategy

### Microcopy
- Be concise
- Use active voice
- Avoid jargon
- Be human
- Guide action

### Labels & Instructions
- Clear and specific
- Explain why (if needed)
- Example formats
- Required vs optional

### Error Messages
- Specific problem
- How to fix
- No technical jargon
- Apologetic when appropriate

## Metrics That Matter

### Engagement
- Time on task
- Completion rate
- Return visits
- Feature adoption

### Satisfaction
- NPS score
- CSAT surveys
- Support tickets
- User feedback

### Performance
- Task success rate
- Error rate
- Time to first action
- Abandonment points

## Design Review Checklist

### Functionality
- [ ] Primary task achievable
- [ ] Edge cases handled
- [ ] Error states designed
- [ ] Loading states designed

### Usability
- [ ] Clear visual hierarchy
- [ ] Obvious next actions
- [ ] Consistent with patterns
- [ ] Minimal cognitive load

### Accessibility
- [ ] Keyboard navigable
- [ ] Screen reader tested
- [ ] Color contrast sufficient
- [ ] Touch targets sized

### Polish
- [ ] Micro-interactions smooth
- [ ] Copy clear and helpful
- [ ] Responsive breakpoints
- [ ] Performance acceptable

## See Also

- Project-specific UX skills for detailed requirements
- `.claude/skills/generic-design-system/` - Visual design
- `CLAUDE.md` - Project constraints

input.content:
# Claude Code Skills Library

Custom skills for Claude Code - reusable across any project.

**Version:** 2.0 (Merged & Generic)
**Last Updated:** December 2025

---

## Generic Skills (Use for Any Project)

### Development Skills

| Skill | Purpose | When to Use |
|-------|---------|-------------|
| **generic-code-reviewer** | Multi-stack code review | Before commits, PR reviews |
| **generic-feature-developer** | Architecture patterns | Adding features, planning changes |
| **generic-design-system** | Design tokens & patterns | UI implementation, styling |
| **generic-ux-designer** | UX best practices | Design reviews, user flows |

### Utility Skills

| Skill | Purpose | When to Use |
|-------|---------|-------------|
| **codebase-documenter** | Generate documentation | README, API docs, comments |
| **tech-debt-analyzer** | Code health analysis | Audits, refactoring planning |
| **test-specialist** | Testing guidance | Writing tests, debugging |
| **frontend-enhancer** | Frontend patterns | UI improvements |
| **document-skills** | Office docs (docx, xlsx, pptx, pdf) | Document creation |
| **seo-analytics-auditor** | SEO analysis | Performance audits |

---

## Project-Specific Skills (Examples)

These skills show how to customize for specific projects:

### tdr-* (thedeadrobot.com)
- **tdr-code-reviewer** - Pure HTML/CSS/JS, cyberpunk theme, <50KB
- **tdr-design-system** - Black/green palette, Roboto font
- **tdr-feature-developer** - Landing page + X automation
- **tdr-ux-designer** - Minimalist constraints

### neuman-brain-* (Neuman Brain App)
- **neuman-brain-code-reviewer** - React/TypeScript, privacy-first
- **neuman-brain-design-system** - Dark mode, accessibility focus
- **neuman-brain-feature-developer** - Local-first architecture
- **neuman-brain-ux-designer** - Productivity app UX

### lazy-golfing-* (Lazy Golfing)
- **lazy-golfing-code-reviewer** - Next.js + NestJS, Prisma
- **lazy-golfing-design-system** - Miami Vice theme
- **lazy-golfing-feature-developer** - Full-stack patterns
- **lazy-golfing-ux-designer** - Golf course app UX

---

## How to Use Skills

### Automatic Activation
Skills activate when relevant context appears in conversation.

### Explicit Invocation
Request skills directly:
```
Use generic-code-reviewer to review this code
Use generic-design-system for the color choices
Use tech-debt-analyzer on this codebase
```

### Skill Tool
```
Skill(generic-code-reviewer)
Skill(tech-debt-analyzer)
```

---

## Skill Structure

Each skill follows this structure:

```
skills/
└── [skill-name]/
    ├── SKILL.md          # Main skill file (required)
    ├── references/       # Reference materials (optional)
    ├── assets/           # Templates, examples (optional)
    └── scripts/          # Helper scripts (optional)
```

### SKILL.md Format

```yaml
---
name: skill-name
description: Brief description for when Claude should use this skill
---

# Skill Title

Content with guidance, rules, patterns, examples...
```

---

## Creating New Skills

### 1. Create Folder
```bash
mkdir -p .claude/skills/[skill-name]
```

### 2. Create SKILL.md
- YAML frontmatter with name and description
- Comprehensive content for Claude to follow
- Examples and patterns

### 3. Add References (Optional)
Place in `references/` folder for additional context.

### 4. Test
Verify skill activates when expected context appears.

---

## Skill Categories

### By Tech Stack

| Stack | Recommended Skills |
|-------|-------------------|
| HTML/CSS/JS | generic-*, tdr-* |
| React/TypeScript | generic-*, neuman-brain-* |
| Next.js/NestJS | generic-*, lazy-golfing-* |
| Python | generic-code-reviewer, tech-debt-analyzer |
| Any | codebase-documenter, test-specialist |

### By Task Type

| Task | Recommended Skills |
|------|-------------------|
| Code review | generic-code-reviewer, [project]-code-reviewer |
| New feature | generic-feature-developer |
| UI/Styling | generic-design-system |
| UX design | generic-ux-designer |
| Documentation | codebase-documenter |
| Code health | tech-debt-analyzer |
| Testing | test-specialist |
| Documents | document-skills |

---

## Skills vs Other Claude Code Features

| Feature | Use For |
|---------|---------|
| **Skills** | Domain knowledge, guidelines, patterns |
| **Slash Commands** | Quick actions, workflows |
| **Hooks** | Automated responses to events |
| **Subagents** | Complex multi-step tasks |
| **MCP Servers** | External tool integration |

---

## Best Practices

### When to Create a Skill
- Repeated guidance needed
- Project-specific standards
- Complex domain knowledge
- Reusable patterns

### When NOT to Create a Skill
- One-time instructions
- Simple preferences (use CLAUDE.md)
- External tool integration (use MCP)

### Skill Content Tips
- Be comprehensive but focused
- Include concrete examples
- Reference other skills when relevant
- Keep updated as project evolves

---

## See Also

- `.claude/automation-guide.md` - Workflow automation
- `.claude/workflow-checklist.md` - Development checklist
- `.claude/commands/` - Custom slash commands
- `.claude/templates/` - Task templates
- `CLAUDE.md` - Project workflow rules

---

## Questions?

- Skills not activating? Check skill name and description match context
- Need new skill? Follow existing patterns in this folder
- Skill conflicts? Review descriptions for proper use cases

| Code review | generic-code-reviewer, [project]-code-reviewer |
| New feature | generic-feature-developer |
| UI/Styling | generic-design-system |
| UX design | generic-ux-designer |
| Documentation | codebase-documenter |
| Code health | tech-debt-analyzer |
| Testing | test-specialist |
| Documents | document-skills |

---

## Skills vs Other Claude Code Features

| Feature | Use For |
|---------|---------|
| **Skills** | Domain knowledge, guidelines, patterns |
| **Slash Commands** | Quick actions, workflows |
| **Hooks** | Automated responses to events |
| **Subagents** | Complex multi-step tasks |
| **MCP Servers** | External tool integration |

---

## Best Practices

### When to Create a Skill
- Repeated guidance needed
- Project-specific standards
- Complex domain knowledge
- Reusable patterns

### When NOT to Create a Skill
- One-time instructions
- Simple preferences (use CLAUDE.md)
- External tool integration (use MCP)

### Skill Content Tips
- Be comprehensive but focused
- Include concrete examples
- Reference other skills when relevant
- Keep updated as project evolves

---

## See Also

- `.claude/automation-guide.md` - Workflow automation
- `.claude/workflow-checklist.md` - Development checklist
- `.claude/commands/` - Custom slash commands
- `.claude/templates/` - Task templates
- `CLAUDE.md` - Project workflow rules

---

## Questions?

- Skills not activating? Check skill name and description match context
- Need new skill? Follow existing patterns in this folder
- Skill conflicts? Review descriptions for proper use cases

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# tdr.portfolio Claude Skills

Custom skills for thedeadrobot landing page and X automation development.

**Last Updated:** December 2025
**Total Skills:** 8 (4 tdr-specific + 4 utility skills)

---

## Project-Specific Skills

### tdr-code-reviewer
**Purpose:** Review code for bugs, security, performance, accessibility, and CLAUDE.md compliance

**When to Use:**
- Before committing code
- Completing features
- Reviewing changes

**Key Checks:**
- CLAUDE.md workflow compliance
- Pure HTML/CSS/JS standards
- Page weight limits (< 50KB)
- Mobile-first design
- Cyberpunk aesthetic consistency
- Python automation code quality

**Invoke:** Before commits, or request: "Review this code"

---

### tdr-design-system
**Purpose:** Complete design system reference - brand colors, typography, components

**When to Use:**
- Implementing UI
- Choosing colors
- Creating animations
- Ensuring brand consistency

**Key Content:**
- Brand colors: Black (#000000), White (#FFFFFF), Green (#00FF32)
- Typography: Roboto font stack
- Components: Title, dropdown menu, GIF container
- Animations: Pulse, stagger, rotate
- Sacred elements (cannot be changed)
- Forbidden changes (user rejected)

**Invoke:** Automatically when UI/styling context appears

---

### tdr-feature-developer
**Purpose:** Guide feature development with architecture patterns

**When to Use:**
- Adding new features
- Modifying automation
- Planning changes

**Key Content:**
- Landing page structure (HTML/CSS/JS)
- X automation system (Python/GitHub Actions)
- Content validation pipeline
- Integration patterns
- Common patterns (social links, topics, validation)

**Invoke:** Request: "Help me develop [feature]"

---

### tdr-ux-designer
**Purpose:** Professional UX expertise within minimalist constraints

**When to Use:**
- Designing new features
- Improving existing UX
- Conducting design reviews

**Key Content:**
- Design thinking for minimal sites
- User psychology principles
- Visual hierarchy
- Accessibility within brand constraints
- Design critique framework

**Invoke:** Request: "Use tdr-ux-designer to critique this design"

---

## Utility Skills

### codebase-documenter
**Purpose:** Create comprehensive documentation

**When to Use:**
- Writing README files
- Creating documentation
- Adding code comments

---

### tech-debt-analyzer
**Purpose:** Identify technical debt and issues

**When to Use:**
- Analyzing codebase health
- Finding code smells
- Checking compliance

---

### test-specialist
**Purpose:** Write and debug tests

**When to Use:**
- Writing tests
- Debugging test failures
- Improving coverage

---

### frontend-enhancer
**Purpose:** Generic frontend enhancement patterns

**⚠️ Note:** tdr.portfolio uses pure HTML/CSS/JS, not Next.js. Use with caution - prefer tdr-specific skills.

---

## Project Context

**Tech Stack:** Pure HTML/CSS/JS (no frameworks, no build tools)
**Aesthetic:** Cyberpunk minimalism - black + neon green
**Purpose:** Landing page (thedeadrobot.com) + X account automation (@thedeadrobot)

**Key Constraints:**
1. No frameworks - Vanilla HTML/CSS/JS only
2. < 50KB page weight - Performance critical
3. Sacred elements - Certain UI elements are locked
4. User approval required - Any visible change needs explicit YES

---

## How Skills Work Together

**Example: Modifying Landing Page**

1. **tdr-feature-developer** → Understand architecture
2. **tdr-ux-designer** → Evaluate change within constraints
3. **tdr-design-system** → Apply colors, spacing
4. **tdr-code-reviewer** → Review before commit

**Example: Modifying Automation**

1. **tdr-feature-developer** → Understand Python/GitHub Actions
2. **tdr-code-reviewer** → Review Python code quality
3. Test with DRY_RUN=true

---

## Skill Invocation

**Automatic:**
Skills activate automatically when relevant context appears

**Explicit:**
Request skills directly:
- "Use tdr-code-reviewer to review this code"
- "Use tdr-design-system to check this styling"
- "Use tdr-ux-designer to critique this layout"

---

## See Also

- `CLAUDE.md` - Complete workflow and rules
- `docs/automation/` - X automation guides
- `docs/brand/` - Brand guidelines
- `.claude/automation-guide.md` - Workflow automation

---

**Questions?**
- Skills not working? Check skill names match exactly
- Need new skill? Follow existing skill patterns
- Skill conflicts? Review skill descriptions for proper use cases

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have comprehensive information. Let me compile this research into a clear, actionable guide.

## MCP Server Lazy Loading Research Summary

Based on my research, here's what you need to know about configuring lazy-loaded MCP servers in Claude Code:

### Current State: No Native Lazy Loading Support Yet

**Important**: Claude Code does **not currently have native lazy-loading support** built-in. All MCP servers are loaded at session startup, consuming context tokens regardless of whether you use them. However, there are several approaches:

---

## Installation Commands for Your 6 MCP Servers

### 1. GitHub MCP
```bash
claude mcp add github --scope user -- docker run -i --rm -e GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN ghcr.io/github/github-mcp-server
```
**Note**: Requires a classic GitHub Personal Access Token (fine-grained tokens don't work for org repos). Set the `GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN` environment variable.

### 2. Playwright MCP
```bash
claude mcp add playwright --scope user -- npx -y @playwright/mcp
```
**Alternative** (if above fails):
```bash
claude mcp add playwright --scope user -- npx -y @playwright/mcp@latest
```
**Note**: You may need to manually install Playwright browsers before running:
```bash
npx playwright install
```

### 3. Sequential Thinking MCP
```bash
claude mcp add sequential-thinking --scope user -- npx -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-sequential-thinking
```

### 4. File System MCP
```bash
claude mcp add filesystem --scope user -- npx -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem /path/to/project /path/to/data
```
**Note**: Replace paths with directories you want Claude to access. You can specify multiple directories.

### 5. PostgreSQL/SQLite MCP
**For PostgreSQL** (using npx):
```bash
claude mcp add postgres --scope user -- npx -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-postgres "postgresql://username:password@hostname:port/database"
```

**For SQLite** (using uv):
```bash
claude mcp add sqlite --scope user -- uv run mcp-server-sqlite --db-path ~/test.db
```

### 6. claude-context MCP
This appears to be a custom/internal server. Based on the pattern, installation would be:
```bash
claude mcp add claude-context --scope user -- npx -y claude-context-mcp
```
*(You may need to verify the exact package name)*

---

## Lazy-Loading Approaches (NOT NATIVE)

Since Claude Code doesn't have native lazy loading yet, here are your options:

### Option A: Use lazy-mcp-preload (Recommended)
**lazy-mcp-preload** is a community proxy that reduces context consumption by ~95% (from 15,000 tokens to 800 tokens for 30 tools):

**Installation**:
```bash
git clone https://github.com/iamsamuelrodda/lazy-mcp-preload.git
cd lazy-mcp-preload
make build
make generate-hierarchy
make deploy
```

**Configuration** (create `~/.claude/lazy-mcp/config/config.json`):
```json
{
  "mcpProxy": {
    "name": "MCP Proxy",
    "type": "stdio",
    "hierarchyPath": "~/.claude/lazy-mcp/hierarchy",
    "options": {
      "lazyLoad": true,
      "preloadAll": true
    }
  },
  "mcpServers": {
    "github": {
      "transportType": "stdio",
      "command": "docker",
      "args": ["run", "-i", "--rm", "-e", "GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN", "ghcr.io/github/github-mcp-server"]
    },
    "playwright": {
      "transportType": "stdio",
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "@playwright/mcp"]
    },
    "sequential-thinking": {
      "transportType": "stdio",
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-sequential-thinking"]
    }
  }
}
```

Then add the proxy as a single MCP server:
```bash
claude mcp add lazy-mcp-proxy --scope user -- ~/.claude/lazy-mcp/lazy-mcp
```

**Key advantage**: `preloadAll: true` starts servers in the background, so no latency on first use.

### Option B: Selective Server Loading
Since lazy loading isn't native, manually control which servers you install:
- Add high-frequency servers (GitHub, Playwright) with `--scope user`
- Add low-frequency servers with `--scope project` and enable them selectively in specific projects
- Use the `/mcp` command to check server status

### Option C: GitHub Issues - Feature Requests
Vote on native lazy-loading support in the official Claude Code repository:
- [Issue #7336](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/7336): "Lazy Loading for MCP Servers and Tools (95% context reduction possible)"
- [Issue #11364](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/11364): "Lazy-load MCP tool definitions to reduce context usage"

---

## Configuration Best Practices

### Store Configuration at User Scope
```bash
# Use --scope user to avoid polluting project directories
claude mcp add github --scope user -- ...
```

This stores servers in `~/.claude.json` rather than `.mcp.json` (which gets checked into git).

### Verify Installation
```bash
claude mcp list
claude mcp get github
```

### Within Claude Code
```
/mcp
```
This shows all connected servers and their status.

### Environment Variables
```bash
# For servers requiring env vars
export GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN=ghp_xxx
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-xxx
claude
```

---

## Known Issues & Workarounds

1. **Playwright installation failures**: Install browsers manually first
   ```bash
   npx playwright install
   ```

2. **GitHub Docker requirement**: Make sure Docker is running
   ```bash
   docker ps  # Check if daemon is active
   ```

3. **Version mismatches**: Use specific versions if `@latest` fails
   ```bash
   claude mcp add playwright --scope user -- npx -y @playwright/mcp@1.48.0
   ```

4. **Windows npm/npx issues**: Use `cmd /c` wrapper
   ```bash
   claude mcp add server --scope user -- cmd /c npx -y package-name
   ```

---

## Recommended Setup for Context Efficiency

Given that native lazy loading isn't available yet, here's my recommendation:

1. **Install lazy-mcp-preload** to act as a proxy that manages all 6 servers with ~95% context reduction
2. **Configure it with `preloadAll: true`** to avoid latency on first tool use
3. **Vote for native support** on the GitHub issues above to encourage official implementation
4. **Monitor the issues** - feature requests have been active and may get implemented in future versions

---

## Resources for Further Reference

toolUseResult.prompt:
Research how to configure MCP servers in Claude Code to be lazy-loaded (not auto-loaded). I need to install these 6 MCP servers but configure them to only load when needed to save context window:
1. GitHub MCP
2. Playwright MCP
3. Sequential Thinking MCP
4. File System MCP
5. PostgreSQL/SQLite MCP
6. claude-context

Find the correct installation commands and configuration options for lazy loading / on-demand loading.

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have comprehensive information. Let me compile this research into a clear, actionable guide.

## MCP Server Lazy Loading Research Summary

Based on my research, here's what you need to know about configuring lazy-loaded MCP servers in Claude Code:

### Current State: No Native Lazy Loading Support Yet

**Important**: Claude Code does **not currently have native lazy-loading support** built-in. All MCP servers are loaded at session startup, consuming context tokens regardless of whether you use them. However, there are several approaches:

---

## Installation Commands for Your 6 MCP Servers

### 1. GitHub MCP
```bash
claude mcp add github --scope user -- docker run -i --rm -e GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN ghcr.io/github/github-mcp-server
```
**Note**: Requires a classic GitHub Personal Access Token (fine-grained tokens don't work for org repos). Set the `GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN` environment variable.

### 2. Playwright MCP
```bash
claude mcp add playwright --scope user -- npx -y @playwright/mcp
```
**Alternative** (if above fails):
```bash
claude mcp add playwright --scope user -- npx -y @playwright/mcp@latest
```
**Note**: You may need to manually install Playwright browsers before running:
```bash
npx playwright install
```

### 3. Sequential Thinking MCP
```bash
claude mcp add sequential-thinking --scope user -- npx -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-sequential-thinking
```

### 4. File System MCP
```bash
claude mcp add filesystem --scope user -- npx -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem /path/to/project /path/to/data
```
**Note**: Replace paths with directories you want Claude to access. You can specify multiple directories.

### 5. PostgreSQL/SQLite MCP
**For PostgreSQL** (using npx):
```bash
claude mcp add postgres --scope user -- npx -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-postgres "postgresql://username:password@hostname:port/database"
```

**For SQLite** (using uv):
```bash
claude mcp add sqlite --scope user -- uv run mcp-server-sqlite --db-path ~/test.db
```

### 6. claude-context MCP
This appears to be a custom/internal server. Based on the pattern, installation would be:
```bash
claude mcp add claude-context --scope user -- npx -y claude-context-mcp
```
*(You may need to verify the exact package name)*

---

## Lazy-Loading Approaches (NOT NATIVE)

Since Claude Code doesn't have native lazy loading yet, here are your options:

### Option A: Use lazy-mcp-preload (Recommended)
**lazy-mcp-preload** is a community proxy that reduces context consumption by ~95% (from 15,000 tokens to 800 tokens for 30 tools):

**Installation**:
```bash
git clone https://github.com/iamsamuelrodda/lazy-mcp-preload.git
cd lazy-mcp-preload
make build
make generate-hierarchy
make deploy
```

**Configuration** (create `~/.claude/lazy-mcp/config/config.json`):
```json
{
  "mcpProxy": {
    "name": "MCP Proxy",
    "type": "stdio",
    "hierarchyPath": "~/.claude/lazy-mcp/hierarchy",
    "options": {
      "lazyLoad": true,
      "preloadAll": true
    }
  },
  "mcpServers": {
    "github": {
      "transportType": "stdio",
      "command": "docker",
      "args": ["run", "-i", "--rm", "-e", "GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN", "ghcr.io/github/github-mcp-server"]
    },
    "playwright": {
      "transportType": "stdio",
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "@playwright/mcp"]
    },
    "sequential-thinking": {
      "transportType": "stdio",
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-sequential-thinking"]
    }
  }
}
```

Then add the proxy as a single MCP server:
```bash
claude mcp add lazy-mcp-proxy --scope user -- ~/.claude/lazy-mcp/lazy-mcp
```

**Key advantage**: `preloadAll: true` starts servers in the background, so no latency on first use.

### Option B: Selective Server Loading
Since lazy loading isn't native, manually control which servers you install:
- Add high-frequency servers (GitHub, Playwright) with `--scope user`
- Add low-frequency servers with `--scope project` and enable them selectively in specific projects
- Use the `/mcp` command to check server status

### Option C: GitHub Issues - Feature Requests
Vote on native lazy-loading support in the official Claude Code repository:
- [Issue #7336](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/7336): "Lazy Loading for MCP Servers and Tools (95% context reduction possible)"
- [Issue #11364](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/11364): "Lazy-load MCP tool definitions to reduce context usage"

---

## Configuration Best Practices

### Store Configuration at User Scope
```bash
# Use --scope user to avoid polluting project directories
claude mcp add github --scope user -- ...
```

This stores servers in `~/.claude.json` rather than `.mcp.json` (which gets checked into git).

### Verify Installation
```bash
claude mcp list
claude mcp get github
```

### Within Claude Code
```
/mcp
```
This shows all connected servers and their status.

### Environment Variables
```bash
# For servers requiring env vars
export GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN=ghp_xxx
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-xxx
claude
```

---

## Known Issues & Workarounds

1. **Playwright installation failures**: Install browsers manually first
   ```bash
   npx playwright install
   ```

2. **GitHub Docker requirement**: Make sure Docker is running
   ```bash
   docker ps  # Check if daemon is active
   ```

3. **Version mismatches**: Use specific versions if `@latest` fails
   ```bash
   claude mcp add playwright --scope user -- npx -y @playwright/mcp@1.48.0
   ```

4. **Windows npm/npx issues**: Use `cmd /c` wrapper
   ```bash
   claude mcp add server --scope user -- cmd /c npx -y package-name
   ```

---

## Recommended Setup for Context Efficiency

Given that native lazy loading isn't available yet, here's my recommendation:

1. **Install lazy-mcp-preload** to act as a proxy that manages all 6 servers with ~95% context reduction
2. **Configure it with `preloadAll: true`** to avoid latency on first tool use
3. **Vote for native support** on the GitHub issues above to encourage official implementation
4. **Monitor the issues** - feature requests have been active and may get implemented in future versions

---

## Resources for Further Reference

- [Claude Code MCP Documentation](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/mcp.md)
- [lazy-mcp-preload Repository](https://github.com/iamsamuelrodda/lazy-mcp-preload)
- [Feature Request #7336](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/7336)
- [Feature Request #11364](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/11364)

---

content.thinking:
Settings files are now merged and identical. Now let me install the MCP servers. Based on the research, native lazy loading isn't available yet in Claude Code, but I set `"enableAllProjectMcpServers": false` which means MCP servers won't auto-enable unless explicitly configured.

For Windows, I need to adjust the commands. Let me install the MCP servers with project scope instead of user scope, which means they'll only be available when working in specific projects rather than globally consuming context.

Actually, let me think about this more carefully:
- `--scope user` = global, available everywhere, always loaded
- `--scope project` = project-specific, only loaded in that project

For context saving, I should install them at project level so they're only loaded when needed in specific projects. But the user might want them available globally...

Let me install them with user scope but document that they will be loaded. The alternative is to use the lazy-mcp-preload proxy which requires building from source.

Actually, the best approach for Windows is:
1. Install the servers with user scope
2. Note that currently all MCP servers are loaded at startup (no native lazy loading)
3. Recommend the lazy-mcp-preload solution if they want to save context

Let me install the servers.

    78→  "cachedChangelog": "# Changelog

## 2.0.69

- Minor bugfixes

## 2.0.68

- Fixed IME (Input Method Editor) support for languages like Chinese, Japanese, and Korean by correctly positioning the composition window at the cursor
- Fixed a bug where disallowed MCP tools were visible to the model
- Fixed an issue where steering messages could be lost while a subagent is working
- Fixed Option+Arrow word navigation treating entire CJK (Chinese, Japanese, Korean) text sequences as a single word instead of navigating by word boundaries
- Improved plan mode exit UX: show simplified yes/no dialog when exiting with empty or missing plan instead of throwing an error
- Add support for enterprise managed settings. Contact your Anthropic account team to enable this feature.

## 2.0.67

- Thinking mode is now enabled by default for Opus 4.5
- Thinking mode configuration has moved to /config
- Added search functionality to `/permissions` command with `/` keyboard shortcut for filtering rules by tool name
- Show reason why autoupdater is disabled in `/doctor`
- Fixed false "Another process is currently updating Claude" error when running `claude update` while another instance is already on the latest version
- Fixed MCP servers from `.mcp.json` being stuck in pending state when running in non-interactive mode (`-p` flag or piped input)
- Fixed scroll position resetting after deleting a permission rule in `/permissions`
- Fixed word deletion (opt+delete) and word navigation (opt+arrow) not working correctly with non-Latin text such as Cyrillic, Greek, Arabic, Hebrew, Thai, and Chinese
- Fixed `claude install --force` not bypassing stale lock files
- Fixed consecutive @~/ file references in CLAUDE.md being incorrectly parsed due to markdown strikethrough interference
- Windows: Fixed plugin MCP servers failing due to colons in log directory paths

## 2.0.65

- Added ability to switch models while writing a prompt using alt+p (linux, windows), option+p (macos).
- Added context window information to status line input
- Added `fileSuggestion` setting for custom `@` file search commands
- Added `CLAUDE_CODE_SHELL` environment variable to override automatic shell detection (useful when login shell differs from actual working shell)
- Fixed prompt not being saved to history when aborting a query with Escape
- Fixed Read tool image handling to identify format from bytes instead of file extension

## 2.0.64

- Made auto-compacting instant
- Agents and bash commands can run asynchronously and send messages to wake up the main agent
- /stats now provides users with interesting CC stats, such as favorite model, usage graph, usage streak
- Added named session support: use `/rename` to name sessions, `/resume <name>` in REPL or `claude --resume <name>` from the terminal to resume them
- Added support for .claude/rules/`.  See https://code.claude.com/docs/en/memory for details.
- Added image dimension metadata when images are resized, enabling accurate coordinate mappings for large images
- Fixed auto-loading .env when using native installer
- Fixed `--system-prompt` being ignored when using `--continue` or `--resume` flags
- Improved `/resume` screen with grouped forked sessions and keyboard shortcuts for preview (P) and rename (R)
- VSCode: Added copy-to-clipboard button on code blocks and bash tool inputs
- VSCode: Fixed extension not working on Windows ARM64 by falling back to x64 binary via emulation
- Bedrock: Improve efficiency of token counting
- Bedrock: Add support for `aws login` AWS Management Console credentials
- Unshipped AgentOutputTool and BashOutputTool, in favor of a new unified TaskOutputTool

## 2.0.62

- Added "(Recommended)" indicator for multiple-choice questions, with the recommended option moved to the top of the list
- Added `attribution` setting to customize commit and PR bylines (deprecates `includeCoAuthoredBy`)
- Fixed duplicate slash commands appearing when ~/.claude is symlinked to a project directory
- Fixed slash command selection not working when multiple commands share the same name
- Fixed an issue where skill files inside symlinked skill directories could become circular symlinks
- Fixed running versions getting removed because lock file incorrectly going stale
- Fixed IDE diff tab not closing when rejecting file changes

## 2.0.61

## 1.0.32

- Updated loopback config for litellm
- Added forceLoginMethod setting to bypass login selection screen

## 1.0.31

- Fixed a bug where ~/.claude.json would get reset when file contained invalid JSON

## 1.0.30

- Custom slash commands: Run bash output, @-mention files, enable thinking with thinking keywords
- Improved file path autocomplete with filename matching
- Added timestamps in Ctrl-r mode and fixed Ctrl-c handling
- Enhanced jq regex support for complex filters with pipes and select

## 1.0.29

- Improved CJK character support in cursor navigation and rendering

## 1.0.28

- Slash commands: Fix selector display during history navigation
- Resizes images before upload to prevent API size limit errors
- Added XDG_CONFIG_HOME support to configuration directory
- Performance optimizations for memory usage
- New attributes (terminal.type, language) in OpenTelemetry logging

## 1.0.27

- Streamable HTTP MCP servers are now supported
- Remote MCP servers (SSE and HTTP) now support OAuth
- MCP resources can now be @-mentioned
- /resume slash command to switch conversations within Claude Code

## 1.0.25

- Slash commands: moved "project" and "user" prefixes to descriptions
- Slash commands: improved reliability for command discovery
- Improved support for Ghostty
- Improved web search reliability

## 1.0.24

- Improved /mcp output
- Fixed a bug where settings arrays got overwritten instead of merged

## 1.0.23

- Released TypeScript SDK: import @anthropic-ai/claude-code to get started
- Released Python SDK: pip install claude-code-sdk to get started

## 1.0.22

- SDK: Renamed `total_cost` to `total_cost_usd`

## 1.0.21

- Improved editing of files with tab-based indentation
- Fix for tool_use without matching tool_result errors
- Fixed a bug where stdio MCP server processes would linger after quitting Claude Code

## 1.0.18

- Added --add-dir CLI argument for specifying additional working directories
- Added streaming input support without require -p flag
- Improved startup performance and session storage performance
- Added CLAUDE_BASH_MAINTAIN_PROJECT_WORKING_DIR environment variable to freeze working directory for bash commands
- Added detailed MCP server tools display (/mcp)
- MCP authentication and permission improvements
- Added auto-reconnection for MCP SSE connections on disconnect
- Fixed issue where pasted content was lost when dialogs appeared

## 1.0.17

- We now emit messages from sub-tasks in -p mode (look for the parent_tool_use_id property)
- Fixed crashes when the VS Code diff tool is invoked multiple times quickly
- MCP server list UI improvements
- Update Claude Code process title to display "claude" instead of "node"

## 1.0.11

- Claude Code can now also be used with a Claude Pro subscription
- Added /upgrade for smoother switching to Claude Max plans
- Improved UI for authentication from API keys and Bedrock/Vertex/external auth tokens
- Improved shell configuration error handling
- Improved todo list handling during compaction

## 1.0.10

- Added markdown table support
- Improved streaming performance

## 1.0.8

- Fixed Vertex AI region fallback when using CLOUD_ML_REGION
- Increased default otel interval from 1s -> 5s
- Fixed edge cases where MCP_TIMEOUT and MCP_TOOL_TIMEOUT weren't being respected
- Fixed a regression where search tools unnecessarily asked for permissions
- Added support for triggering thinking non-English languages
- Improved compacting UI

## 1.0.7

- Renamed /allowed-tools -> /permissions
- Migrated allowedTools and ignorePatterns from .claude.json -> settings.json
- Deprecated claude config commands in favor of editing settings.json
- Fixed a bug where --dangerously-skip-permissions sometimes didn't work in --print mode
- Improved error handling for /install-github-app
- Bugfixes, UI polish, and tool reliability improvements

## 1.0.6

- Improved edit reliability for tab-indented files
- Respect CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR everywhere
- Reduced unnecessary tool permission prompts
- Added support for symlinks in @file typeahead
- Bugfixes, UI polish, and tool reliability improvements

## 1.0.4

- Fixed a bug where MCP tool errors weren't being parsed correctly

## 1.0.1

- Added `DISABLE_INTERLEAVED_THINKING` to give users the option to opt out of interleaved thinking.
- Improved model references to show provider-specific names (Sonnet 3.7 for Bedrock, Sonnet 4 for Console)
- Updated documentation links and OAuth process descriptions

## 1.0.0

- Claude Code is now generally available
- Introducing Sonnet 4 and Opus 4 models

## 0.2.125

- Breaking change: Bedrock ARN passed to `ANTHROPIC_MODEL` or `ANTHROPIC_SMALL_FAST_MODEL` should no longer contain an escaped slash (specify `/` instead of `%2F`)
- Removed `DEBUG=true` in favor of `ANTHROPIC_LOG=debug`, to log all requests

## 0.2.117

- Breaking change: --print JSON output now returns nested message objects, for forwards-compatibility as we introduce new metadata fields
- Introduced settings.cleanupPeriodDays
- Introduced CLAUDE_CODE_API_KEY_HELPER_TTL_MS env var
- Introduced --debug mode

## 0.2.108

- You can now send messages to Claude while it works to steer Claude in real-time
- Introduced BASH_DEFAULT_TIMEOUT_MS and BASH_MAX_TIMEOUT_MS env vars
- Fixed a bug where thinking was not working in -p mode
- Fixed a regression in /cost reporting
- Deprecated MCP wizard interface in favor of other MCP commands
- Lots of other bugfixes and improvements

## 0.2.107

- CLAUDE.md files can now import other files. Add @path/to/file.md to ./CLAUDE.md to load additional files on launch

## 0.2.106

- MCP SSE server configs can now specify custom headers
- Fixed a bug where MCP permission prompt didn't always show correctly

## 0.2.105

- Claude can now search the web
- Moved system & account status to /status
- Added word movement keybindings for Vim
- Improved latency for startup, todo tool, and file edits

## 0.2.102

- Improved thinking triggering reliability
- Improved @mention reliability for images and folders
- You can now paste multiple large chunks into one prompt

## 0.2.100

- Fixed a crash caused by a stack overflow error
- Made db storage optional; missing db support disables --continue and --resume

## 0.2.98

- Fixed an issue where auto-compact was running twice

## 0.2.96

- Claude Code can now also be used with a Claude Max subscription (https://claude.ai/upgrade)

## 0.2.93

- Resume conversations from where you left off from with "claude --continue" and "claude --resume"
- Claude now has access to a Todo list that helps it stay on track and be more organized

## 0.2.82

- Added support for --disallowedTools
- Renamed tools for consistency: LSTool -> LS, View -> Read, etc.

## 0.2.75

- Hit Enter to queue up additional messages while Claude is working
- Drag in or copy/paste image files directly into the prompt
- @-mention files to directly add them to context
- Run one-off MCP servers with `claude --mcp-config <path-to-file>`
- Improved performance for filename auto-complete

## 0.2.74

- Added support for refreshing dynamically generated API keys (via apiKeyHelper), with a 5 minute TTL
- Task tool can now perform writes and run bash commands

## 0.2.72

- Updated spinner to indicate tokens loaded and tool usage

## 0.2.70

- Network commands like curl are now available for Claude to use
- Claude can now run multiple web queries in parallel
- Pressing ESC once immediately interrupts Claude in Auto-accept mode

## 0.2.69

- Fixed UI glitches with improved Select component behavior
- Enhanced terminal output display with better text truncation logic

## 0.2.67

- Shared project permission rules can be saved in .claude/settings.json

## 0.2.66

- Print mode (-p) now supports streaming output via --output-format=stream-json
- Fixed issue where pasting could trigger memory or bash mode unexpectedly

## 0.2.63

- Fixed an issue where MCP tools were loaded twice, which caused tool call errors

## 0.2.61

- Navigate menus with vim-style keys (j/k) or bash/emacs shortcuts (Ctrl+n/p) for faster interaction
- Enhanced image detection for more reliable clipboard paste functionality
- Fixed an issue where ESC key could crash the conversation history selector

## 0.2.59

- Copy+paste images directly into your prompt
- Improved progress indicators for bash and fetch tools
- Bugfixes for non-interactive mode (-p)

## 0.2.54

- Quickly add to Memory by starting your message with '#'
- Press ctrl+r to see full output for long tool results
- Added support for MCP SSE transport

## 0.2.53

- New web fetch tool lets Claude view URLs that you paste in
- Fixed a bug with JPEG detection

## 0.2.50

- New MCP "project" scope now allows you to add MCP servers to .mcp.json files and commit them to your repository

## 0.2.49

- Previous MCP server scopes have been renamed: previous "project" scope is now "local" and "global" scope is now "user"

## 0.2.47

- Press Tab to auto-complete file and folder names
- Press Shift + Tab to toggle auto-accept for file edits
- Automatic conversation compaction for infinite conversation length (toggle with /config)

## 0.2.44

- Ask Claude to make a plan with thinking mode: just say 'think' or 'think harder' or even 'ultrathink'

## 0.2.41

- MCP server startup timeout can now be configured via MCP_TIMEOUT environment variable
- MCP server startup no longer blocks the app from starting up

## 0.2.37

- New /release-notes command lets you view release notes at any time
- `claude config add/remove` commands now accept multiple values separated by commas or spaces

## 0.2.36

- Import MCP servers from Claude Desktop with `claude mcp add-from-claude-desktop`
- Add MCP servers as JSON strings with `claude mcp add-json <n> <json>`

## 0.2.34

- Vim bindings for text input - enable with /vim or /config

## 0.2.32

- Interactive MCP setup wizard: Run "claude mcp add" to add MCP servers with a step-by-step interface
- Fix for some PersistentShell issues

## 0.2.31

- Custom slash commands: Markdown files in .claude/commands/ directories now appear as custom slash commands to insert prompts into your conversation
- MCP debug mode: Run with --mcp-debug flag to get more information about MCP server errors

## 0.2.30

- Added ANSI color theme for better terminal compatibility
- Fixed issue where slash command arguments weren't being sent properly
- (Mac-only) API keys are now stored in macOS Keychain

## 0.2.26

- New /approved-tools command for managing tool permissions
- Word-level diff display for improved code readability
- Fuzzy matching for slash commands

## 0.2.21

- Fuzzy matching for /commands
