## 2.0.69

- Minor bugfixes

## 2.0.68

- Fixed IME (Input Method Editor) support for languages like Chinese, Japanese, and Korean by correctly positioning the composition window at the cursor
- Fixed a bug where disallowed MCP tools were visible to the model
- Fixed an issue where steering messages could be lost while a subagent is working
- Fixed Option+Arrow word navigation treating entire CJK (Chinese, Japanese, Korean) text sequences as a single word instead of navigating by word boundaries
- Improved plan mode exit UX: show simplified yes/no dialog when exiting with empty or missing plan instead of throwing an error
- Add support for enterprise managed settings. Contact your Anthropic account team to enable this feature.

## 2.0.67

- Thinking mode is now enabled by default for Opus 4.5
- Thinking mode configuration has moved to /config
- Added search functionality to `/permissions` command with `/` keyboard shortcut for filtering rules by tool name
- Show reason why autoupdater is disabled in `/doctor`
- Fixed false "Another process is currently updating Claude" error when running `claude update` while another instance is already on the latest version
- Fixed MCP servers from `.mcp.json` being stuck in pending state when running in non-interactive mode (`-p` flag or piped input)
- Fixed scroll position resetting after deleting a permission rule in `/permissions`
- Fixed word deletion (opt+delete) and word navigation (opt+arrow) not working correctly with non-Latin text such as Cyrillic, Greek, Arabic, Hebrew, Thai, and Chinese
- Fixed `claude install --force` not bypassing stale lock files
- Fixed consecutive @~/ file references in CLAUDE.md being incorrectly parsed due to markdown strikethrough interference
- Windows: Fixed plugin MCP servers failing due to colons in log directory paths

## 2.0.65

- Added ability to switch models while writing a prompt using alt+p (linux, windows), option+p (macos).
- Added context window information to status line input
- Added `fileSuggestion` setting for custom `@` file search commands
- Added `CLAUDE_CODE_SHELL` environment variable to override automatic shell detection (useful when login shell differs from actual working shell)
- Fixed prompt not being saved to history when aborting a query with Escape
- Fixed Read tool image handling to identify format from bytes instead of file extension

## 2.0.64

- Made auto-compacting instant
- Agents and bash commands can run asynchronously and send messages to wake up the main agent
- /stats now provides users with interesting CC stats, such as favorite model, usage graph, usage streak
- Added named session support: use `/rename` to name sessions, `/resume <name>` in REPL or `claude --resume <name>` from the terminal to resume them
- Added support for .claude/rules/`.  See https://code.claude.com/docs/en/memory for details.
- Added image dimension metadata when images are resized, enabling accurate coordinate mappings for large images
- Fixed auto-loading .env when using native installer
- Fixed `--system-prompt` being ignored when using `--continue` or `--resume` flags
- Improved `/resume` screen with grouped forked sessions and keyboard shortcuts for preview (P) and rename (R)
- VSCode: Added copy-to-clipboard button on code blocks and bash tool inputs
- VSCode: Fixed extension not working on Windows ARM64 by falling back to x64 binary via emulation
- Bedrock: Improve efficiency of token counting
- Bedrock: Add support for `aws login` AWS Management Console credentials
- Unshipped AgentOutputTool and BashOutputTool, in favor of a new unified TaskOutputTool

## 2.0.62

- Added "(Recommended)" indicator for multiple-choice questions, with the recommended option moved to the top of the list
- Added `attribution` setting to customize commit and PR bylines (deprecates `includeCoAuthoredBy`)
- Fixed duplicate slash commands appearing when ~/.claude is symlinked to a project directory
- Fixed slash command selection not working when multiple commands share the same name
- Fixed an issue where skill files inside symlinked skill directories could become circular symlinks
- Fixed running versions getting removed because lock file incorrectly going stale
- Fixed IDE diff tab not closing when rejecting file changes

## 2.0.61

- Reverted VSCode support for multiple terminal clients due to responsiveness issues.

## 2.0.60

- Added background agent support. Agents run in the background while you work
- Added --disable-slash-commands CLI flag to disable all slash commands
- Added model name to "Co-Authored-By" commit messages
- Enabled "/mcp enable [server-name]" or "/mcp disable [server-name]" to quickly toggle all servers
- Updated Fetch to skip summarization for pre-approved websites
- VSCode: Added support for multiple terminal clients connecting to the IDE server simultaneously

## 2.0.59

- Added --agent CLI flag to override the agent setting for the current session
- Added `agent` setting to configure main thread with a specific agent's system prompt, tool restrictions, and model
- VS Code: Fixed .claude.json config file being read from incorrect location

## 2.0.58

- Pro users now have access to Opus 4.5 as part of their subscription!
- Fixed timer duration showing "11m 60s" instead of "12m 0s"
- Windows: Managed settings now prefer `C:\Program Files\ClaudeCode` if it exists. Support for `C:\ProgramData\ClaudeCode` will be removed in a future version.

## 2.0.57

- Added feedback input when rejecting plans, allowing users to tell Claude what to change
- VSCode: Added streaming message support for real-time response display

## 2.0.56

- Added setting to enable/disable terminal progress bar (OSC 9;4)
- VSCode Extension: Added support for VS Code's secondary sidebar (VS Code 1.97+), allowing Claude Code to be displayed in the right sidebar while keeping the file explorer on the left. Requires setting sidebar as Preferred Location in the config.

## 2.0.55

- Fixed proxy DNS resolution being forced on by default. Now opt-in via `CLAUDE_CODE_PROXY_RESOLVES_HOSTS=true` environment variable
- Fixed keyboard navigation becoming unresponsive when holding down arrow keys in memory location selector
- Improved AskUserQuestion tool to auto-submit single-select questions on the last question, eliminating the extra review screen for simple question flows
- Improved fuzzy matching for `@` file suggestions with faster, more accurate results

## 2.0.54

- Hooks: Enable PermissionRequest hooks to process 'always allow' suggestions and apply permission updates
- Fix issue with excessive iTerm notifications

## 2.0.52

- Fixed duplicate message display when starting Claude with a command line argument
- Fixed `/usage` command progress bars to fill up as usage increases (instead of showing remaining percentage)
- Fixed image pasting not working on Linux systems running Wayland (now falls back to wl-paste when xclip is unavailable)
- Permit some uses of `$!` in bash commands

## 2.0.51

- Added Opus 4.5! https://www.anthropic.com/news/claude-opus-4-5
- Introducing Claude Code for Desktop: https://claude.com/download
- To give you room to try out our new model, we've updated usage limits for Claude Code users. See the Claude Opus 4.5 blog for full details
- Pro users can now purchase extra usage for access to Opus 4.5 in Claude Code
- Plan Mode now builds more precise plans and executes more thoroughly
- Usage limit notifications now easier to understand
- Switched `/usage` back to "% used"
- Fixed handling of thinking errors
- Fixed performance regression

## 2.0.50

- Fixed bug preventing calling MCP tools that have nested references in their input schemas
- Silenced a noisy but harmless error during upgrades
- Improved ultrathink text display
- Improved clarity of 5-hour session limit warning message

## 2.0.49

- Added readline-style ctrl-y for pasting deleted text
- Improved clarity of usage limit warning message
- Fixed handling of subagent permissions

## 2.0.47

- Improved error messages and validation for `claude --teleport`
- Improved error handling in `/usage`
- Fixed race condition with history entry not getting logged at exit
- Fixed Vertex AI configuration not being applied from `settings.json`

- Fixed image files being reported with incorrect media type when format cannot be detected from metadata

## 2.0.36

- Fixed: DISABLE_AUTOUPDATER environment variable now properly disables package manager update notifications

## 2.0.35

- Improve fuzzy search results when searching commands
- Improved VS Code extension to respect `chat.fontSize` and `chat.fontFamily` settings throughout the entire UI, and apply font changes immediately without requiring reload
- Added `CLAUDE_CODE_EXIT_AFTER_STOP_DELAY` environment variable to automatically exit SDK mode after a specified idle duration, useful for automated workflows and scripts
- Migrated `ignorePatterns` from project config to deny permissions in the localSettings.

## 2.0.34

- VSCode Extension: Added setting to configure the initial permission mode for new conversations
- Improved file path suggestion performance with native Rust-based fuzzy finder
- Fixed infinite token refresh loop that caused MCP servers with OAuth (e.g., Slack) to hang during connection
- Fixed memory crash when reading or writing large files (especially base64-encoded images)

## 2.0.33

- Native binary installs now launch quicker.
- Fixed `claude doctor` incorrectly detecting Homebrew vs npm-global installations by properly resolving symlinks
- Fixed `claude mcp serve` exposing tools with incompatible outputSchemas

## 2.0.32

- Un-deprecate output styles based on community feedback
- Added `companyAnnouncements` setting for displaying announcements on startup

## 2.0.31

- Windows: native installation uses shift+tab as shortcut for mode switching, instead of alt+m

## 2.0.30

- Added helpful hint to run `security unlock-keychain` when encountering API key errors on macOS with locked keychain
- Added `allowUnsandboxedCommands` sandbox setting to disable the dangerouslyDisableSandbox escape hatch at policy level

## 1.0.65

- IDE: Fixed connection stability issues and error handling for diagnostics
- Windows: Fixed shell environment setup for users without .bashrc files

## 1.0.64

- Agents: Added model customization support - you can now specify which model an agent should use
- Agents: Fixed unintended access to the recursive agent tool
- Hooks: Added systemMessage field to hook JSON output for displaying warnings and context
- SDK: Fixed user input tracking across multi-turn conversations
- Added hidden files to file search and @-mention suggestions

## 1.0.63

- Windows: Fixed file search, @agent mentions, and custom slash commands functionality

## 1.0.62

- Added @-mention support with typeahead for custom agents. @<your-custom-agent> to invoke it
- Hooks: Added SessionStart hook for new session initialization
- /add-dir command now supports typeahead for directory paths
- Improved network connectivity check reliability

## 1.0.61

- Transcript mode (Ctrl+R): Changed Esc to exit transcript mode rather than interrupt
- Settings: Added `--settings` flag to load settings from a JSON file
- Settings: Fixed resolution of settings files paths that are symlinks
- OTEL: Fixed reporting of wrong organization after authentication changes
- Slash commands: Fixed permissions checking for allowed-tools with Bash
- IDE: Added support for pasting images in VSCode MacOS using ⌘+V
- IDE: Added `CLAUDE_CODE_AUTO_CONNECT_IDE=false` for disabling IDE auto-connection
- Added `CLAUDE_CODE_SHELL_PREFIX` for wrapping Claude and user-provided shell commands run by Claude Code

## 1.0.60

- You can now create custom subagents for specialized tasks! Run /agents to get started

## 1.0.59

- SDK: Added tool confirmation support with canUseTool callback
- SDK: Allow specifying env for spawned process
- Hooks: Exposed PermissionDecision to hooks (including "ask")
- Hooks: UserPromptSubmit now supports additionalContext in advanced JSON output
- Fixed issue where some Max users that specified Opus would still see fallback to Sonnet

## 1.0.58

- Added support for reading PDFs
- MCP: Improved server health status display in 'claude mcp list'
- Hooks: Added CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR env var for hook commands

## 1.0.57

- Added support for specifying a model in slash commands
- Improved permission messages to help Claude understand allowed tools
- Fix: Remove trailing newlines from bash output in terminal wrapping

## 1.0.56

- Windows: Enabled shift+tab for mode switching on versions of Node.js that support terminal VT mode
- Fixes for WSL IDE detection
- Fix an issue causing awsRefreshHelper changes to .aws directory not to be picked up

## 1.0.55

- Clarified knowledge cutoff for Opus 4 and Sonnet 4 models
- Windows: fixed Ctrl+Z crash
- SDK: Added ability to capture error logging
- Add --system-prompt-file option to override system prompt in print mode

## 1.0.54

- Hooks: Added UserPromptSubmit hook and the current working directory to hook inputs
- Custom slash commands: Added argument-hint to frontmatter
- Windows: OAuth uses port 45454 and properly constructs browser URL
- Windows: mode switching now uses alt + m, and plan mode renders properly
- Shell: Switch to in-memory shell snapshot to fix file-related errors

## 1.0.53

- Updated @-mention file truncation from 100 lines to 2000 lines
- Add helper script settings for AWS token refresh: awsAuthRefresh (for foreground operations like aws sso login) and awsCredentialExport (for background operation with STS-like response).

## 1.0.52

- Added support for MCP server instructions

## 1.0.51

- Added support for native Windows (requires Git for Windows)
- Added support for Bedrock API keys through environment variable AWS_BEARER_TOKEN_BEDROCK
- Settings: /doctor can now help you identify and fix invalid setting files
- `--append-system-prompt` can now be used in interactive mode, not just --print/-p.
- Increased auto-compact warning threshold from 60% to 80%
- Fixed an issue with handling user directories with spaces for shell snapshots
- OTEL resource now includes os.type, os.version, host.arch, and wsl.version (if running on Windows Subsystem for Linux)
- Custom slash commands: Fixed user-level commands in subdirectories
- Plan mode: Fixed issue where rejected plan from sub-task would get discarded

## 1.0.48

- Fixed a bug in v1.0.45 where the app would sometimes freeze on launch
- Added progress messages to Bash tool based on the last 5 lines of command output
- Added expanding variables support for MCP server configuration
- Moved shell snapshots from /tmp to ~/.claude for more reliable Bash tool calls
- Improved IDE extension path handling when Claude Code runs in WSL
- Hooks: Added a PreCompact hook
- Vim mode: Added c, f/F, t/T

## 1.0.45

- Redesigned Search (Grep) tool with new tool input parameters and features
- Disabled IDE diffs for notebook files, fixing "Timeout waiting after 1000ms" error
- Fixed config file corruption issue by enforcing atomic writes
- Updated prompt input undo to Ctrl+\_ to avoid breaking existing Ctrl+U behavior, matching zsh's undo shortcut
- Stop Hooks: Fixed transcript path after /clear and fixed triggering when loop ends with tool call
- Custom slash commands: Restored namespacing in command names based on subdirectories. For example, .claude/commands/frontend/component.md is now /frontend:component, not /component.

## 1.0.44

- New /export command lets you quickly export a conversation for sharing
- MCP: resource_link tool results are now supported
- MCP: tool annotations and tool titles now display in /mcp view
- Changed Ctrl+Z to suspend Claude Code. Resume by running `fg`. Prompt input undo is now Ctrl+U.

## 1.0.43

- Fixed a bug where the theme selector was saving excessively
- Hooks: Added EPIPE system error handling

## 1.0.42

- Added tilde (`~`) expansion support to `/add-dir` command

## 1.0.41

- Hooks: Split Stop hook triggering into Stop and SubagentStop
- Hooks: Enabled optional timeout configuration for each command
- Hooks: Added "hook_event_name" to hook input
- Fixed a bug where MCP tools would display twice in tool list
- New tool parameters JSON for Bash tool in `tool_decision` event

## 1.0.40

- Fixed a bug causing API connection errors with UNABLE_TO_GET_ISSUER_CERT_LOCALLY if `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS` was set

## 1.0.39

- New Active Time metric in OpenTelemetry logging

## 1.0.38

- Released hooks. Special thanks to community input in https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/712. Docs: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks

## 1.0.37

- Remove ability to set `Proxy-Authorization` header via ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN or apiKeyHelper

## 1.0.36

- Web search now takes today's date into context
- Fixed a bug where stdio MCP servers were not terminating properly on exit

## 1.0.35

- Added support for MCP OAuth Authorization Server discovery

## 1.0.34

- Fixed a memory leak causing a MaxListenersExceededWarning message to appear

## 1.0.33

- Improved logging functionality with session ID support
- Added prompt input undo functionality (Ctrl+Z and vim 'u' command)
- Improvements to plan mode

## 1.0.32

- Updated loopback config for litellm
- Added forceLoginMethod setting to bypass login selection screen

## 1.0.31

- Fixed a bug where ~/.claude.json would get reset when file contained invalid JSON

## 1.0.30

- Custom slash commands: Run bash output, @-mention files, enable thinking with thinking keywords
- Improved file path autocomplete with filename matching
- Added timestamps in Ctrl-r mode and fixed Ctrl-c handling
- Enhanced jq regex support for complex filters with pipes and select

## 1.0.29

- Improved CJK character support in cursor navigation and rendering

## 1.0.28

- Slash commands: Fix selector display during history navigation
- Resizes images before upload to prevent API size limit errors
- Added XDG_CONFIG_HOME support to configuration directory
- Performance optimizations for memory usage
- New attributes (terminal.type, language) in OpenTelemetry logging

## 1.0.27

- Streamable HTTP MCP servers are now supported
- Remote MCP servers (SSE and HTTP) now support OAuth
- MCP resources can now be @-mentioned
- /resume slash command to switch conversations within Claude Code

## 1.0.25

- Slash commands: moved "project" and "user" prefixes to descriptions
- Slash commands: improved reliability for command discovery
- Improved support for Ghostty
- Improved web search reliability

## 1.0.24

- Improved /mcp output
- Fixed a bug where settings arrays got overwritten instead of merged

## 1.0.23

- Released TypeScript SDK: import @anthropic-ai/claude-code to get started
- Released Python SDK: pip install claude-code-sdk to get started

## 1.0.22

- SDK: Renamed `total_cost` to `total_cost_usd`

## 1.0.21

- Improved editing of files with tab-based indentation
- Fix for tool_use without matching tool_result errors
- Fixed a bug where stdio MCP server processes would linger after quitting Claude Code

## 1.0.18

- Added --add-dir CLI argument for specifying additional working directories
- Added streaming input support without require -p flag
- Improved startup performance and session storage performance
- Added CLAUDE_BASH_MAINTAIN_PROJECT_WORKING_DIR environment variable to freeze working directory for bash commands
- Added detailed MCP server tools display (/mcp)
- MCP authentication and permission improvements
- Added auto-reconnection for MCP SSE connections on disconnect
- Fixed issue where pasted content was lost when dialogs appeared

## 1.0.17

- We now emit messages from sub-tasks in -p mode (look for the parent_tool_use_id property)
- Fixed crashes when the VS Code diff tool is invoked multiple times quickly
- MCP server list UI improvements
- Update Claude Code process title to display "claude" instead of "node"

## 1.0.11

- Claude Code can now also be used with a Claude Pro subscription
- Added /upgrade for smoother switching to Claude Max plans
- Improved UI for authentication from API keys and Bedrock/Vertex/external auth tokens
- Improved shell configuration error handling
- Improved todo list handling during compaction

## 1.0.10

- Added markdown table support
- Improved streaming performance

## 1.0.8

- Fixed Vertex AI region fallback when using CLOUD_ML_REGION
- Increased default otel interval from 1s -> 5s
- Fixed edge cases where MCP_TIMEOUT and MCP_TOOL_TIMEOUT weren't being respected
- Fixed a regression where search tools unnecessarily asked for permissions
- Added support for triggering thinking non-English languages
- Improved compacting UI

## 1.0.7

- Renamed /allowed-tools -> /permissions
- Migrated allowedTools and ignorePatterns from .claude.json -> settings.json
- Deprecated claude config commands in favor of editing settings.json
- Fixed a bug where --dangerously-skip-permissions sometimes didn't work in --print mode
- Improved error handling for /install-github-app
- Bugfixes, UI polish, and tool reliability improvements

## 1.0.6

- Improved edit reliability for tab-indented files
- Respect CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR everywhere
- Reduced unnecessary tool permission prompts
- Added support for symlinks in @file typeahead
- Bugfixes, UI polish, and tool reliability improvements

## 1.0.4

- Fixed a bug where MCP tool errors weren't being parsed correctly

## 1.0.1

- Added `DISABLE_INTERLEAVED_THINKING` to give users the option to opt out of interleaved thinking.
- Improved model references to show provider-specific names (Sonnet 3.7 for Bedrock, Sonnet 4 for Console)
- Updated documentation links and OAuth process descriptions

## 1.0.0

- Claude Code is now generally available
- Introducing Sonnet 4 and Opus 4 models

## 0.2.125

- Breaking change: Bedrock ARN passed to `ANTHROPIC_MODEL` or `ANTHROPIC_SMALL_FAST_MODEL` should no longer contain an escaped slash (specify `/` instead of `%2F`)
- Removed `DEBUG=true` in favor of `ANTHROPIC_LOG=debug`, to log all requests

## 0.2.117

- Breaking change: --print JSON output now returns nested message objects, for forwards-compatibility as we introduce new metadata fields
- Introduced settings.cleanupPeriodDays
- Introduced CLAUDE_CODE_API_KEY_HELPER_TTL_MS env var
- Introduced --debug mode

## 0.2.108

- You can now send messages to Claude while it works to steer Claude in real-time
- Introduced BASH_DEFAULT_TIMEOUT_MS and BASH_MAX_TIMEOUT_MS env vars
- Fixed a bug where thinking was not working in -p mode
- Fixed a regression in /cost reporting
- Deprecated MCP wizard interface in favor of other MCP commands
- Lots of other bugfixes and improvements

## 0.2.107

- CLAUDE.md files can now import other files. Add @path/to/file.md to ./CLAUDE.md to load additional files on launch

## 0.2.106

- MCP SSE server configs can now specify custom headers
- Fixed a bug where MCP permission prompt didn't always show correctly

## 0.2.105

- Claude can now search the web
- Moved system & account status to /status
- Added word movement keybindings for Vim
- Improved latency for startup, todo tool, and file edits

## 0.2.102

- Improved thinking triggering reliability
- Improved @mention reliability for images and folders
- You can now paste multiple large chunks into one prompt

## 0.2.100

- Fixed a crash caused by a stack overflow error
- Made db storage optional; missing db support disables --continue and --resume

## 0.2.98

- Fixed an issue where auto-compact was running twice

## 0.2.96

- Claude Code can now also be used with a Claude Max subscription (https://claude.ai/upgrade)

## 0.2.93

- Resume conversations from where you left off from with "claude --continue" and "claude --resume"
- Claude now has access to a Todo list that helps it stay on track and be more organized

## 0.2.82

- Added support for --disallowedTools
- Renamed tools for consistency: LSTool -> LS, View -> Read, etc.

## 0.2.75

- Hit Enter to queue up additional messages while Claude is working
- Drag in or copy/paste image files directly into the prompt
- @-mention files to directly add them to context
- Run one-off MCP servers with `claude --mcp-config <path-to-file>`
- Improved performance for filename auto-complete

## 0.2.74

- Added support for refreshing dynamically generated API keys (via apiKeyHelper), with a 5 minute TTL
- Task tool can now perform writes and run bash commands

## 0.2.72

- Updated spinner to indicate tokens loaded and tool usage

## 0.2.70

- Network commands like curl are now available for Claude to use
- Claude can now run multiple web queries in parallel
- Pressing ESC once immediately interrupts Claude in Auto-accept mode

## 0.2.69

- Fixed UI glitches with improved Select component behavior
- Enhanced terminal output display with better text truncation logic

## 0.2.67

- Shared project permission rules can be saved in .claude/settings.json

## 0.2.66

- Print mode (-p) now supports streaming output via --output-format=stream-json
- Fixed issue where pasting could trigger memory or bash mode unexpectedly

## 0.2.63

- Fixed an issue where MCP tools were loaded twice, which caused tool call errors

## 0.2.61

- Navigate menus with vim-style keys (j/k) or bash/emacs shortcuts (Ctrl+n/p) for faster interaction
- Enhanced image detection for more reliable clipboard paste functionality
- Fixed an issue where ESC key could crash the conversation history selector

## 0.2.59

- Copy+paste images directly into your prompt
- Improved progress indicators for bash and fetch tools
- Bugfixes for non-interactive mode (-p)

## 0.2.54

- Quickly add to Memory by starting your message with '#'
- Press ctrl+r to see full output for long tool results
- Added support for MCP SSE transport

## 0.2.53

- New web fetch tool lets Claude view URLs that you paste in
- Fixed a bug with JPEG detection

## 0.2.50

- New MCP "project" scope now allows you to add MCP servers to .mcp.json files and commit them to your repository

## 0.2.49

- Previous MCP server scopes have been renamed: previous "project" scope is now "local" and "global" scope is now "user"

## 0.2.47

- Press Tab to auto-complete file and folder names
- Press Shift + Tab to toggle auto-accept for file edits
- Automatic conversation compaction for infinite conversation length (toggle with /config)

## 0.2.44

- Ask Claude to make a plan with thinking mode: just say 'think' or 'think harder' or even 'ultrathink'

## 0.2.41

- MCP server startup timeout can now be configured via MCP_TIMEOUT environment variable
- MCP server startup no longer blocks the app from starting up

## 0.2.37

- New /release-notes command lets you view release notes at any time
- `claude config add/remove` commands now accept multiple values separated by commas or spaces

## 0.2.36

- Import MCP servers from Claude Desktop with `claude mcp add-from-claude-desktop`
- Add MCP servers as JSON strings with `claude mcp add-json <n> <json>`

## 0.2.34

- Vim bindings for text input - enable with /vim or /config

## 0.2.32

- Interactive MCP setup wizard: Run "claude mcp add" to add MCP servers with a step-by-step interface
- Fix for some PersistentShell issues

## 0.2.31

- Custom slash commands: Markdown files in .claude/commands/ directories now appear as custom slash commands to insert prompts into your conversation
- MCP debug mode: Run with --mcp-debug flag to get more information about MCP server errors

## 0.2.30

- Added ANSI color theme for better terminal compatibility
- Fixed issue where slash command arguments weren't being sent properly
- (Mac-only) API keys are now stored in macOS Keychain

## 0.2.26

- New /approved-tools command for managing tool permissions
- Word-level diff display for improved code readability
- Fuzzy matching for slash commands

## 0.2.21

- Fuzzy matching for /commands

content.text:
---

# MCP Servers Installation Complete

## Settings Files

Both `settings.json` and `settings.local.json` are now **identical** with:
- Combined permissions from both files
- All plugins enabled consistently  
- `enableAllProjectMcpServers: false` to prevent auto-loading project MCP servers

## MCP Servers Installed

| Server | Purpose | Status | Notes |
|--------|---------|--------|-------|
| **sequential-thinking** | Structured problem-solving | Connected | Works |
| **playwright** | E2E testing, web automation | Failed | May need `npx playwright install` first |
| **filesystem** | Enhanced file operations | Failed | May need configuration |
| **sqlite** | Database queries | Failed | May need database path |
| **github** | GitHub integration | Failed | Needs `GITHUB_TOKEN` env var |
| **memory** | Persistent context | Failed | May need initialization |

## Important: Native Lazy Loading Not Available Yet

**Claude Code doesn't have native lazy loading for MCP servers.** All installed MCP servers load at session startup, consuming context tokens. However, I've configured optimizations:

1. **`enableAllProjectMcpServers: false`** - Project-level MCP servers won't auto-enable
2. **Servers can be toggled** - Use `/mcp enable [server]` or `/mcp disable [server]` 
3. **@mention to toggle** - Type `@server-name` to enable/disable servers

## To Fix Failed Servers

```bash
# For Playwright (install browsers)
npx playwright install

# For GitHub (set token)
set GITHUB_TOKEN=ghp_your_token_here

# For SQLite (may need database path)
# Edit .claude.json and add database path to args

# For filesystem (already configured for C:/Users/tjn)
# May need to restart Claude Code
```

## Context-Saving Alternatives

If context window is a major concern, there's a community solution:

**lazy-mcp-preload** - Reduces context by ~95% (15,000 tokens → 800 tokens for 30 tools)
- GitHub: https://github.com/iamsamuelrodda/lazy-mcp-preload
- Requires building from source

## Current Configuration in `.claude.json`

```json
"mcpServers": {
  "sequential-thinking": { ... },
  "playwright": { ... },
  "filesystem": { ... },
  "sqlite": { ... },
  "github": { ... },
  "memory": { ... }
}
```

## Summary

| Item | Before | After |
|------|--------|-------|
| settings.json | Different from local | Identical |
| settings.local.json | Different from global | Identical |
| MCP servers installed | 0 | 6 |
| enableAllProjectMcpServers | true | false |
| Native lazy loading | Not available | Not available (pending feature) |

**Note:** You may need to restart Claude Code for the MCP servers to reconnect properly. Some servers require environment variables or additional setup.

content.text:
All servers are working. Here's the summary:

---

# MCP Servers Summary

## 1. Sequential Thinking
**Package:** `@modelcontextprotocol/server-sequential-thinking`

**Purpose:** Structured problem-solving and reasoning

**Capabilities:**
- Break down complex problems into sequential steps
- Maintain reasoning chains across multiple turns
- Track thought progression and dependencies
- Ideal for multi-step analysis, debugging, and planning

**Headless:** Yes (stdio-based, no UI)

---

## 2. Playwright
**Package:** `@playwright/mcp@latest` (v1.57.0)

**Purpose:** Browser automation and E2E testing

**Capabilities:**
- Navigate to URLs and interact with web pages
- Click, type, scroll, and interact with elements
- Take screenshots and capture page content
- Execute JavaScript in browser context
- Test across Chromium, Firefox, WebKit
- Fill forms, handle dialogs, manage cookies

**Headless:** Yes (runs headless by default via MCP)

---

## 3. Filesystem
**Package:** `@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem`

**Purpose:** Enhanced file system operations

**Capabilities:**
- Read/write files within allowed directories
- List directory contents
- Create/delete files and directories
- Search for files by pattern
- Get file metadata (size, dates, permissions)
- **Scope:** Currently configured for `C:/Users/tjn`

**Headless:** Yes (stdio-based, no UI)

---

## 4. GitHub
**Package:** `@modelcontextprotocol/server-github`

**Purpose:** GitHub API integration

**Capabilities:**
- Create, read, update issues and PRs
- Manage repositories (create, fork, clone info)
- Search code, issues, repos, users
- Read file contents from repos
- List branches, commits, releases
- Manage labels, milestones, projects

**Headless:** Yes (API-based, no UI)

**Note:** For full functionality, set `GITHUB_TOKEN` environment variable with a Personal Access Token.

---

## 5. Memory
**Package:** `@modelcontextprotocol/server-memory`

**Purpose:** Persistent knowledge storage

**Capabilities:**
- Store key-value pairs that persist across sessions
- Create entities with properties and relationships
- Build knowledge graphs
- Query stored information
- Associate context with specific topics
- Maintain long-term memory between conversations

**Headless:** Yes (stdio-based, no UI)

---

## 6. SQLite
**Package:** `mcp-server-sqlite` (Python/uvx)

**Purpose:** Database operations

**Capabilities:**
- Create and manage SQLite databases
- Execute SQL queries (SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE)
- Create tables and schemas
- Run analytical queries
- Export query results
- Manage database migrations

**Headless:** Yes (stdio-based, no UI)

---

## Quick Reference

| Server | Primary Use | Headless |
|--------|-------------|----------|
| **sequential-thinking** | Complex reasoning, step-by-step analysis | Yes |
| **playwright** | Web automation, testing, screenshots | Yes |
| **filesystem** | File operations in C:/Users/tjn | Yes |
| **github** | GitHub repos, issues, PRs, code search | Yes |
| **memory** | Persistent storage, knowledge graphs | Yes |
| **sqlite** | Database queries, data management | Yes |

All servers are **fully headless** and operate via stdio protocol - no browser windows or GUIs will open during normal operation.

Also, if I give you a GitHub API key - can you add it where it needs to live so I can just include that when I copy this folder around?

content.thinking:
The user wants:
1. The filesystem MCP server to be scoped to wherever the `.claude/` folder exists (dynamic path based on current working directory)
2. To add a GitHub API key that can be stored in the `.claude/` folder so it's portable

For the filesystem scope - I can use a relative path like `.` or `./` to indicate the current working directory, or I can remove the hardcoded path entirely.

For the GitHub token - I can configure the MCP server to use an environment variable, and the user can set that env var or I can configure it in the MCP server's env settings. However, storing the actual token in a file in `.claude/` would be a security risk if they're copying this folder around and potentially committing it to git.

Let me think about the best approach:

1. **Filesystem scope**: Change from `C:/Users/tjn` to `.` (current directory) or remove the path argument to make it use the current working directory.

2. **GitHub token**: The MCP server can read from `GITHUB_TOKEN` or `GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN` environment variable. I could:
   - Add it to the MCP server's `env` config in `.claude.json`
   - Create a `.env` file in `.claude/` that gets loaded
   - Store in a credentials file

The safest portable approach would be to:
1. Configure the GitHub MCP server to look for a token in a specific env var
2. Have the user create a `.claude/.env` or similar file with the token
3. Make sure that file is in `.gitignore`

But wait - the `.claude.json` file is in the user's home directory, not in the project's `.claude/` folder. The MCP server configs are stored in `~/.claude.json`, not in the project folder.

Let me reconsider:
- The `.claude/` folder in the user's home (`C:\Users\tjn\.claude\`) contains settings, skills, commands, etc.


- Project-level configurations require a different strategy for portability
- Environment variables offer a flexible solution for token management
- Ensuring the token remains secure and not accidentally committed is crucial

I'll explore project-specific configuration options that maintain security and flexibility for the GitHub token and filesystem scoping.

content.text:
All servers connected. Filesystem now uses `.` (current working directory).

## Important Note About Portability

**MCP server configs live in `~/.claude.json` (your home directory), NOT in the `.claude/` project folder.**

The `.claude/` folder you're organizing contains:
- Skills, commands, templates, settings

But MCP server configs are stored separately in `C:\Users\tjn\.claude.json`.

### For True Portability, You Have Two Options:

**Option A: Project-level MCP configs (Recommended)**

Create a `.mcp.json` file in each project root:
```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "filesystem": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem", "."]
    },
    "github": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-github"],
      "env": {
        "GITHUB_TOKEN": "${GITHUB_TOKEN}"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

**Option B: Environment variable for GitHub token**

Set `GITHUB_TOKEN` as a system environment variable - the GitHub MCP will read it automatically.

---

## For GitHub Token

Please share your GitHub Personal Access Token and I'll configure it. You can either:

1. **Give me the token** - I'll add it to the MCP server config (stored in `~/.claude.json`)
2. **Set it as environment variable** - More secure, works across all projects:
   ```bash
   # Windows (permanent)
   setx GITHUB_TOKEN "ghp_your_token_here"
   
   # Or add to your shell profile
   ```

content.content:
# Claude Code 2.0 Cheatsheet - Comprehensive Extract

## Installation Methods
- **curl (macOS/Linux)**: `curl -fsSL https://claude.ai/install.sh | bash`
- **Homebrew**: `brew install --cask claude-code`
- **PowerShell (Windows)**: `irm https://claude.ai/install.ps1 | iex`
- **npm**: `npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code`

## Essential Keyboard Shortcuts
| Shortcut | Function |
|----------|----------|
| `!` | Bash mode prefix |
| `@` | Mention files/folders |
| `Esc` | Interrupt Claude |
| `Esc+Esc` | Open rewind menu |
| `Ctrl+R` | Full output/context |
| `Shift+Tab` | Auto-accept ("yolo mode") |
| `Shift+Tab+Tab` | Plan mode |
| `Ctrl+V` | Paste image |

## Core Slash Commands
- `/add-dir` - Add working directories
- `/agents` - Manage AI subagents
- `/clear` - Clear conversation history
- `/config` - View/modify configuration
- `/help` - Get usage help
- `/mcp` - Manage MCP servers
- `/rewind` - Undo changes
- `/sandbox` - Enable sandboxed bash
- `/vim` - Enter vim mode

## Configuration Hierarchy (Priority Order)
1. Enterprise: `/etc/claude-code/managed-settings.json`
2. Project Local: `.claude/settings.local.json`
3. Project Shared: `.claude/settings.json`
4. User Global: `~/.claude/settings.json`

## Checkpointing Features
- Automatic tracking of file edits
- **Limitations**: Bash changes and external edits not tracked
- Checkpoint every user prompt
- Options: Conversation rewind, code rewind, or full rewind

## Headless Mode (Automation)
```bash
claude -p "query"                    # Non-interactive
claude -p --output-format json "query"  # JSON output
claude -c -p "query"               # Continue session
echo "query" | claude -p            # Piped input
```

## Agent Skills Structure
- **Location**: `.claude/skills/` (project) or `~/.claude/skills/` (personal)
- Model-invoked (automatic discovery)
- Includes SKILL.md + optional scripts
- Can limit tool access per skill

## MCP Server Management
```bash
claude mcp add <name> <command> [args...]
claude mcp list
claude mcp remove <name>
```

Popular integrations: GitHub, Linear, Notion, Figma, Stripe, Vercel, Asana, HubSpot

## Git Worktrees Benefits
- Preserves Claude context per worktree
- Enables parallel development
- Safe experimentation without context loss
- Supports model comparison testing

## Subagents
- **Purpose**: Specialized AI assistants with separate contexts
- **Locations**: `.claude/agents/` (project) or `~/.claude/agents/` (personal)
- Can grant specific tool access
- Claude proactively uses appropriate subagents

## Permission Templates

**Basic Security**:
```json
{
  "permissions": {
    "allow": ["Bash(npm run *)", "Bash(git status)"],
    "deny": ["Read(./.env*)", "Bash(rm -rf:*)"]
  }
}
```

**Strict Security** (production):
- Ask for all Bash, Write, Edit
- Deny env files, credentials, WebFetch, curl/wget/ssh

## Hooks - Automation Events
- `PreToolUse` - Block tool calls
- `PostToolUse` - Run after tool calls
- `UserPromptSubmit` - Before processing
- `SessionStart/SessionEnd` - Session lifecycle
- `PreCompact` - Before conversation compacting

## Custom Slash Commands
Store templates in:
- Project: `.claude/commands/`
- Personal: `~/.claude/commands/`

Supports `$ARGUMENTS`, `$1`, `$2` for parameters and `` !`command` `` for bash execution.

## File Location Shortcuts
- Project local config: `.claude/settings.local.json`
- Team settings: `.claude/settings.json`
- User settings: `~/.claude/settings.json`

## Output Formats
- `text` - Plain text (default)
- `json` - Structured with metadata
- `stream-json` - Real-time streaming

## Key Management Commands
- `claude config list` - View all settings
- `claude config get <key>` - Check specific value
- `claude config set <key> <value>` - Change setting
- `/hooks` - Open hooks interface
- `/plugin` - Plugin management

Here are the key findings on Claude Code hooks and automation workflows from 2025:

## Overview

Hooks are a very recent addition to Claude Code, released initially in June 2025.(no content)Claude Code hooks are user-defined shell commands that execute at various points in Claude Code's lifecycle. This feature provides control over Claude Code's behavior, ensuring certain actions always happen rather than relying on the LLM to choose to run them.

## Hook Types

Claude Code hooks are shell commands that execute at various points in Claude Code's lifecycle - like PreToolUse (before tool execution), PostToolUse (after tool completion), Notification (when Claude sends notifications), and Stop (when Claude finishes responding).

## Key Tips & Best Practices

### Configuration
Settings can be stored in `.claude/settings.json` for project settings that can be committed for your team, or `.claude/settings.local.json` for local project settings.

### Automation Examples
- You can automatically format TypeScript files after editing using PostToolUse hooks with matchers like "Edit|Write".
- You can add a hook to make Claude send you a desktop notification when a session is completed using the Stop hook, which fires when everything in a session is done.

### Workflow Management
To keep automated tasks running smoothly, break down your approach into: Sequencing (run tasks step-by-step, ensuring dependencies complete before the next begins) and Parallelizing (execute independent tasks simultaneously, shaving time off the workflow).

### Integration Tips
- Integrate Claude commands into your Git hooks or CI pipelines for early feedback loops. Document your workflows in CLAUDE.md to keep automation sustainable as your team grows.
- Claude Code includes headless mode for non-interactive contexts like CI, pre-commit hooks, build scripts, and automation. Use the -p flag with a prompt to enable headless mode, and --output-format stream-json for streaming JSON output.

### Easy Setup
You can have Claude build hooks for you. You can ask Claude to add default hooks, commands, and settings.(no content)You can also use the interactive /hooks command in Claude Code to configure hooks through a menu interface, which is often easier than editing JSON directly.

### Security Consideration
Malicious hooks code can exfiltrate your data. Always review your hooks implementation before registering them.

## Top MCP Servers for Claude Code (2025)

Based on the search results, here are the most recommended MCP servers:

### Most Popular MCP Servers

The top 10 MCP servers for Claude Code in 2025 include GitHub, Apidog, and PostgreSQL, which help boost productivity, automate tasks, and streamline workflows.

**Key servers mentioned across sources:**

1. **GitHub MCP Server** - Transforms Claude Code into a comprehensive development platform by enabling direct interaction with repositories, issues, pull requests, and CI/CD workflows, eliminating friction of context switching between terminal and GitHub's web interface.

2. **Sequential Thinking MCP** - Revolutionizes how Claude Code approaches complex problem-solving by introducing a structured, reflective thinking process that mirrors human cognitive patterns, enabling Claude to methodically work through problems.

3. **Brave Search MCP** - For web research; **Context7** for documentation; **Puppeteer** for web automation; and **Reddit MCP** for community insights.

4. **Playwright MCP Server** - Revolutionizes web automation and testing by providing Claude Code with the ability to interact with web applications, essential for developers building end-to-end tests and automating web workflows.

5. **Zapier MCP** - Connects Claude to thousands of other apps with one MCP server, facilitating secure interactions between Claude and other apps you use.

### Claude Code Plugins (Official)

Claude Code now supports plugins: custom collections of slash commands, agents, MCP servers, and hooks that install with a single command.(no content)Plugins are a lightweight way to package and share slash commands, subagents, MCP servers, and hooks. You can install plugins directly within Claude Code using the /plugin command, now in public beta.

### Extensions & Resources

There are 50+ Claude Code extensions, addons, MCP servers, and IDE integrations available for 2025, including curated collections with links and descriptions.(no content)Notable tools include: GitHub MCP Server, PostgreSQL MCP, File System MCP, SQLite MCP, Claude Command Suite (119+ professional slash commands), and Memory MCP for persistent knowledge graphs.

### MCP Growth Statistics

The estimated number of total MCP server downloads was just under 100,000 in November 2024, which increased to 8 million by April 2025.

Links: [{"title":"Claude Code: Best practices for agentic coding","url":"https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/claude-code-best-practices"},{"title":"GitHub - Cranot/claude-code-guide: Claude Code Comprehensive Guide","url":"https://github.com/Cranot/claude-code-guide"},{"title":"GitHub - hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code: [FOR USERS HAVING PERFORMANCE ISSUES: USE README_BACKUP INSTEAD] A curated list of awesome commands, files, and workflows for Claude Code","url":"https://github.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code"},{"title":"GitHub - zebbern/claude-code-guide: Full guide on claude tips and tricks and how you can optimise your claude code the best & strive to find every command possible even hidden ones!","url":"https://github.com/zebbern/claude-code-guide"},{"title":"Using CLAUDE.MD files: Customizing Claude Code for your codebase | Claude","url":"https://claude.com/blog/using-claude-md-files"},{"title":"GitHub - vincenthopf/claude-code: A curated list of commands, files, and workflows for Claude Code","url":"https://github.com/vincenthopf/claude-code"},{"title":"My Claude Code Usage Best Practices and Recommendations | N+1 Blog","url":"https://nikiforovall.blog/productivity/2025/06/13/claude-code-rules.html"},{"title":"GitHub - awattar/claude-code-best-practices: 💻 Best practices and examples for using Claude Code - Anthropic’s terminal-native AI - for writing, editing, and refactoring code with deep project awareness, prompt design, and safe automation.","url":"https://github.com/awattar/claude-code-best-practices"},{"title":"Cooking with Claude Code: The Complete Guide - Sid Bharath","url":"https://www.siddharthbharath.com/claude-code-the-complete-guide/"},{"title":"ClaudeLog - Claude Code Docs, Guides, Tutorials & Best Practices","url":"https://claudelog.com/"}]

Here are the search results for Claude Code CLAUDE.md best practices:

## Official Anthropic Resources

"CLAUDE.md files solve this by giving Claude persistent context about your project. Think of it as a configuration file that Claude automatically incorporates into every conversation, ensuring it always knows your project structure, coding standards, and preferred workflows."(no content)"As you work with Claude Code, use the # key to add instructions you find yourself repeating—these additions accumulate into a CLAUDE.md that genuinely reflects how your team works." Treat customization as an ongoing practice rather than a one-time setup task, and a well-maintained CLAUDE.md evolves with your codebase.(no content)CLAUDE.md is a special file that Claude automatically pulls into context.

## GitHub Community Resources

Several curated GitHub repositories offer best practices:

1. **[awesome-claude-code](https://github.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code)** - "Collection of best practices, tips, and techniques for Claude Code development workflows, enhanced with distributable plugins." It's well-documented and adaptable to different projects and workflows.

2. **[claude-code-guide](https://github.com/Cranot/claude-code-guide)** - Explains why CLAUDE.md helps: "Provides context immediately at session start, reduces need to re-explain project structure, stores project-specific patterns and conventions, documents what works (and what doesn't), shared with team via git." Claude Code will automatically read it if present at project root.

3. **[claude-code-best-practices](https://github.com/awattar/claude-code-best-practices)** - Includes a `/custom-init` command that "automatically generates comprehensive CLAUDE.md files for any project by analyzing project structure, technology stack, and development patterns." The repo covers best practices for writing, editing, and refactoring code with deep project awareness.

4. **[vincenthopf/claude-code](https://github.com/vincenthopf/claude-code)** - A curated list of slash-commands, CLAUDE.md files, CLI tools, and other resources. CLAUDE.md files "contain important guidelines and context-specific information or instructions that help Claude Code to better understand your project and your coding standards."

## Best Practices Summary

Use the `/init` command to initialize the CLAUDE.md file. There's no required format for CLAUDE.md files - keep it concise and human-readable. You can use it to store important information about your project, such as architecture, design decisions, and other relevant details.(no content)Project memory lives at `./CLAUDE.md` (share it with your team), while user memory is at `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` (personal preferences). You can use `# <text>` to add particular memory to the CLAUDE.md file.(no content)One practitioner found success by iterating on CLAUDE.md "to refine it into modules of task context, rules, numbered steps and examples which lead Claude to success. This became the foundation for many of the Claude Code best practices and optimization techniques."

Based on the search results, here's what I found about SuperClaude framework for Claude Code configuration:

## SuperClaude Framework Overview

SuperClaude is a meta-programming configuration framework that transforms Claude Code into a structured development platform through behavioral instruction injection and component orchestration.

There are multiple repositories and versions available:

### Main Repositories

1. **SuperClaude-Org/SuperClaude_Framework** (GitHub)
   - Version 4.1 focuses on stabilizing the slash command architecture, enhancing agent capabilities, and improving documentation.
   - After installation, includes 30 commands including `/sc:research` for deep web research enhanced with Tavily MCP.

2. **NomenAK/SuperClaude** (Original)
   - SuperClaude v2 introduces architectural improvements including streamlined @include reference system for configuration management, and 9 cognitive personas integrated into the flag system (--persona-architect, --persona-security, etc.).

### Key Features

- SuperClaude is an open-source framework that extends Claude Code with 19 specialized commands, 9 cognitive personas, and evidence-based development workflows.

- SuperClaude includes a 70% token-reduction pipeline that manages larger, complex projects efficiently.

- It provides 16 specialized commands for common dev tasks, smart personas that pick the right expert for different domains, MCP server integration for docs, UI components, and browser automation, and task management.

### Installation

Installation is straightforward via git clone and shell script:
```bash
git clone https://github.com/NomenAK/SuperClaude.git
cd SuperClaude
./install.sh
```

It installs to ~/.claude/ by default with zero dependencies. After installation, all configuration files are located in ~/.claude/ (your home directory), not in the project directory.

### Available Commands

Commands include categories like Development (/sc:implement, /sc:build, /sc:design), Analysis (/sc:analyze, /sc:troubleshoot, /sc:explain), Quality (/sc:improve, /sc:test, /sc:cleanup), and Others (/sc:document, /sc:git, /sc:estimate, /sc:task, /sc:index, /sc:load, /sc:spawn).

### Platform Support

SuperClaude supports Linux, macOS, and Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL). The installer automatically detects your platform and configures accordingly.

Here are the search results for Claude Code subagents and custom agents configuration in 2025:

## Overview

Custom subagents in Claude Code are specialized AI assistants that can be invoked to handle specific types of tasks. They enable more efficient problem-solving by providing task-specific configurations with customized system prompts, tools and a separate context window.(no content)Released by Anthropic in July 2025, this feature addresses the critical problem of "context pollution" and marked a watershed moment—enabling specialized AI assistants to work collaboratively on complex projects while maintaining independent contexts and expertise areas.

## Configuration Basics

Agents are defined using simple Markdown files containing a YAML frontmatter block. Claude scans two directories: Project scope creates subagents in the `.claude/agents` directory, while personal scope creates them in the `~/.claude/agents` directory.(no content)The basic structure is:
```yaml
---
name: subagent-name
description: When this agent should be invoked
tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep
---
You are a [role description and expertise areas]...
```

## Creating Subagents

If you select "Generate with Claude," it will generate the subagent's description and system prompt based on the prompt you enter. If you select "Manual configuration," you will enter the description and system prompt yourself.(no content)After Claude generates the configuration, you'll be prompted to choose your available tools. The default setting is to inherit the tools used in the parent session. Finally, you can select a color to assign to the subagent, which will be displayed when you call it in the main conversation session.

## Tool Permissions by Agent Type

Each subagent's tools field specifies Claude Code built-in tools optimized for their role:
- **Read-only agents** (reviewers, auditors): Read, Grep, Glob - analyze without modifying
- **Research agents** (analysts, researchers): Read, Grep, Glob, WebFetch, WebSearch - gather information
- **Code writers** (developers, engineers): Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep - create and execute

## Built-in Subagents

The Explore subagent is a fast, lightweight agent optimized for searching and analyzing codebases. It operates in strict read-only mode with Bash commands limited to ls, git status, git log, git diff, find, cat, head, tail.(no content)The Plan subagent is only used in plan mode. In normal execution mode, Claude uses the general-purpose agent or other custom subagents you've created.

## Best Practices

Start with Claude-generated agents: Anthropic highly recommends generating your initial subagent with Claude and then iterating on it. This approach gives you the best results - a solid foundation that you can customize.(no content)Design focused subagents: Create subagents with single, clear responsibilities rather than trying to make one subagent do everything. This improves performance and makes subagents more predictable.(no content)Limit tool access: Only grant tools that are necessary for the subagent's purpose. This improves security and helps the subagent focus on relevant actions.

## Key Benefits

Subagents enable parallelization—you can spin up multiple subagents to work on different tasks simultaneously. They also help manage context: subagents use their own isolated context windows, and only send relevant information back to the orchestrator. This makes them ideal for tasks that require sifting through large amounts of information.(no content)Custom agents are specialized agents that can be utilized to solve specific tasks. They are automatically invoked by Claude in a similar manner to how Tools are invoked automatically.

## Community Resources

The VoltAgent community maintains a collection of Claude Code subagents—specialized AI agents designed for specific development tasks including 100+ production-ready agents for full-stack development, DevOps, data science, and business operations.

## Configuration Basics

Agents are defined using simple Markdown files containing a YAML frontmatter block. Claude scans two directories: Project scope creates subagents in the `.claude/agents` directory, while personal scope creates them in the `~/.claude/agents` directory.(no content)The basic structure is:
```yaml
---
name: subagent-name
description: When this agent should be invoked
tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep
---
You are a [role description and expertise areas]...
```

## Creating Subagents

If you select "Generate with Claude," it will generate the subagent's description and system prompt based on the prompt you enter. If you select "Manual configuration," you will enter the description and system prompt yourself.(no content)After Claude generates the configuration, you'll be prompted to choose your available tools. The default setting is to inherit the tools used in the parent session. Finally, you can select a color to assign to the subagent, which will be displayed when you call it in the main conversation session.

## Tool Permissions by Agent Type

Each subagent's tools field specifies Claude Code built-in tools optimized for their role:
- **Read-only agents** (reviewers, auditors): Read, Grep, Glob - analyze without modifying
- **Research agents** (analysts, researchers): Read, Grep, Glob, WebFetch, WebSearch - gather information
- **Code writers** (developers, engineers): Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep - create and execute

## Built-in Subagents

The Explore subagent is a fast, lightweight agent optimized for searching and analyzing codebases. It operates in strict read-only mode with Bash commands limited to ls, git status, git log, git diff, find, cat, head, tail.(no content)The Plan subagent is only used in plan mode. In normal execution mode, Claude uses the general-purpose agent or other custom subagents you've created.

## Best Practices

Start with Claude-generated agents: Anthropic highly recommends generating your initial subagent with Claude and then iterating on it. This approach gives you the best results - a solid foundation that you can customize.(no content)Design focused subagents: Create subagents with single, clear responsibilities rather than trying to make one subagent do everything. This improves performance and makes subagents more predictable.(no content)Limit tool access: Only grant tools that are necessary for the subagent's purpose. This improves security and helps the subagent focus on relevant actions.

## Key Benefits

Subagents enable parallelization—you can spin up multiple subagents to work on different tasks simultaneously. They also help manage context: subagents use their own isolated context windows, and only send relevant information back to the orchestrator. This makes them ideal for tasks that require sifting through large amounts of information.(no content)Custom agents are specialized agents that can be utilized to solve specific tasks. They are automatically invoked by Claude in a similar manner to how Tools are invoked automatically.

## Community Resources

The VoltAgent community maintains a collection of Claude Code subagents—specialized AI agents designed for specific development tasks including 100+ production-ready agents for full-stack development, DevOps, data science, and business operations.

From setup, prompt engineering, commands, hooks, workflows, automation, and integrations, to MCP servers, tools, and the BMAD method—packed with step-by-step tutorials, real-world examples, and expert strategies to make this the global go-to repo for Claude mastery.","url":"https://github.com/wesammustafa/Claude-Code-Everything-You-Need-to-Know"}]

Here are the search results for Claude Code CLAUDE.md examples and best practices on GitHub in 2025:

## Official Anthropic Resources

CLAUDE.md files solve the challenge of giving AI coding agents enough context by giving Claude persistent context about your project. Think of it as a configuration file that Claude automatically incorporates into every conversation, ensuring it always knows your project structure, coding standards, and preferred workflows.(no content)Think of /init as a starting point, not a finished product. The generated CLAUDE.md captures obvious patterns but may miss nuances specific to your workflow. Review what Claude produces and refine it based on your team's actual practices.(no content)As you work with Claude Code, use the # key to add instructions you find yourself repeating—these additions accumulate into a CLAUDE.md that genuinely reflects how your team works.

## Key GitHub Resources

1. **[Cranot/claude-code-guide](https://github.com/Cranot/claude-code-guide)** - CLAUDE.md provides context immediately at session start, reduces need to re-explain project structure, stores project-specific patterns and conventions, documents what works (and what doesn't), and is shared with team via git. Claude Code will automatically read it if present at project root.

2. **[hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code](https://github.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code)** - A tailored workflow for enabling automated UI/UX design review, including specialized sub agents, slash commands, CLAUDE.md excerpts, and more.

3. **[awattar/claude-code-best-practices](https://github.com/awattar/claude-code-best-practices)** - Includes /custom-init which automatically generates comprehensive CLAUDE.md files for any project by analyzing project structure, technology stack, and development patterns. Also provides /help-commands for comprehensive help documentation for all available custom commands.

4. **[zebbern/claude-code-guide](https://github.com/zebbern/claude-code-guide)** - Keep durable context in CLAUDE.md at the project root. In the REPL, use /memory to manage it and @path to import file content into prompts.

## Best Practices

Use the /init command to initialize the CLAUDE.md file. There's no required format for CLAUDE.md files. Keep it concise and human-readable. You can use it to store important information about your project, such as architecture, design decisions, and other relevant details that can help Claude understand your codebase better.(no content)Project memory is stored at ./CLAUDE.md (share it with your team), while user memory goes at ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md (personal preferences).(no content)The most powerful feature is the CLAUDE.md file system. You can place these files in two locations: Project-level (./CLAUDE.md in your repository root) and Global (~/.claude/CLAUDE.md in your home directory). Keep project-specific architectural decisions, coding standards, and deployment instructions in the project file, while the global file contains general development preferences and workflow patterns.(no content)Your CLAUDE.md files become part of Claude's prompts, so they should be refined like any frequently used prompt. A common mistake is adding extensive content without iterating on its effectiveness. Take time to experiment and determine what produces the best instruction following from the model.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Links: [{"title":"Feature Request: Add Hooks for Git Workflow Automation (`PreCommit`, `PostCommit`) · Issue #4834 · anthropics/claude-code","url":"https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/4834"},{"title":"Demystifying Claude Code Hooks | Aaron Brethorst","url":"https://www.brethorsting.com/blog/2025/08/demystifying-claude-code-hooks/"},{"title":"Claude Code Hooks | GitButler Docs","url":"https://docs.gitbutler.com/features/ai-integration/claude-code-hooks"},{"title":"Automate Your AI Workflows with Claude Code Hooks | Butler's Log","url":"https://blog.gitbutler.com/automate-your-ai-workflows-with-claude-code-hooks"},{"title":"Claude Code: Best practices for agentic coding","url":"https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/claude-code-best-practices"},{"title":"Get started with Claude Code hooks - Claude Code Docs","url":"https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks-guide"},{"title":"Claude Code Hooks: What is and How to Use It - CometAPI - All AI Models in One API","url":"https://www.cometapi.com/claude-code-hooks-what-is-and-how-to-use-it/"},{"title":"Basic Claude Code | Harper Reed's Blog","url":"https://harper.blog/2025/05/08/basic-claude-code/"},{"title":"Claude Code: Part 8 - Hooks for Automated Quality Checks | Luiz Tanure","url":"https://www.letanure.dev/blog/2025-08-06--claude-code-part-8-hooks-automated-quality-checks"},{"title":"Guide to Claude Code Subagents & Hooks for Automation","url":"https://www.arsturn.com/blog/a-beginners-guide-to-using-subagents-and-hooks-in-claude-code"}]

Here's what I found about Claude Code hooks automation with pre-commit and post-commit examples from 2025:

## Overview

Anthropic's Claude Code Hooks represent a significant advancement in AI-driven development workflows, enabling deterministic extension and customization of Claude Code's behavior. Released on June 30, 2025, this feature empowers developers to inject custom shell commands at specific lifecycle events.(no content)Claude Code Hooks are user-defined shell commands or scripts that execute automatically at predetermined points in Claude Code's workflow. Unlike ad-hoc model prompts or manual triggers, Claude Code Hooks guarantee that specific operations—such as linting, formatting, notifications, or logging—occur consistently without additional user intervention.

## Pre-Commit Hook Examples

### Running Linters and Tests Before Git Commits
You can create a realistic hook that runs Rubocop and Rspec before any git commit. The script runs Rubocop with auto-correction, and only proceeds to run tests if the linter passes.(no content)The configuration uses a `PreToolUse` matcher for Bash commands that checks if the command starts with "git commit": the hook extracts the command field using jq and only runs the pre-commit checks script when a git commit is detected.

### Configuration Locations
You have two options for hook configuration: Global settings at `~/.claude/settings.json` (for universal behaviors) or Project settings at `.claude/settings.json` (for project-specific automation). Project settings are ideal for commands like "run a linter on any changed *.rb files before committing" and travel with your repository.

## Feature Request for Native Git Hooks

The current hooks system in Claude Code is powerful for automating tasks based on tool usage (e.g., PreToolUse, PostToolUse). However, there is no direct way to deterministically trigger actions immediately before or after a git commit operation.(no content)The current workaround is to use a PostToolUse hook with a matcher for `Bash(git commit:*)`. This is suboptimal because it cannot block a commit that fails a pre-check, it runs after the commit command has been attempted, and parsing the command to extract commit details is complex and brittle.

### Proposed PreCommit Hook Use Cases:
- Automatic Ticket Association (scanning branch names to prepend ticket IDs to commit messages) and adding Co-author Information (automatically appending the `Co-authored-by: Claude` trailer).
- Secret Scanning to detect accidentally included API keys and blocking commits if found, and checking for TODOs/FIXMEs to prevent commits with unfinished work.
- Linting & Formatting to automatically run tools like ESLint or Prettier on staged files, and running unit tests to block commits if tests fail.

### PostCommit Hook
A PostCommit hook runs after the commit has been successfully created, making it ideal for automation, notification, and synchronization tasks.

## Practical Examples

### GitButler Integration
If you are using Claude Code, you can use the hooks functionality to manage the output of even multiple simultaneous instances, while isolating all the generated code into virtual or stacked branches automatically. In this case, there is no need to set up the MCP server, as the hooks will handle everything.

### Auto-Formatting TypeScript Files
Example use cases for hooks include automatic formatting: run prettier on .ts files, gofmt on .go files, etc. after every file edit.

### Blocking Protected Files
You can create hooks to block modifications to sensitive files like `.env`, `package-lock.json`, or `.git/` directories using Python scripts that check the file path and return exit code 2 to block the operation.

## Headless Mode for CI/Pre-commit
Claude Code includes headless mode for non-interactive contexts like CI, pre-commit hooks, build scripts, and automation. Use the `-p` flag with a prompt to enable headless mode, and `--output-format stream-json` for streaming JSON output.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Features flexible layouts, real-time cost tracking, MCP monitoring, prayer times, and beautiful themes.","url":"https://github.com/rz1989s/claude-code-statusline"},{"title":"How I’m Using Claude Code Status Line (New Feature) To Keep Context | by Joe Njenga | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@joe.njenga/how-im-using-claude-code-status-line-new-feature-to-keep-context-96a4adf21728"},{"title":"GitHub - gabriel-dehan/claude_monitor_statusline: A customizable status line for Claude Code that displays usage info","url":"https://github.com/gabriel-dehan/claude_monitor_statusline"},{"title":"Custom Claude Code Statusline: Track Context and Current Directory","url":"https://1ar.io/p/custom-claude-code-statusline-track-context-and-current-directory/"},{"title":"Feature: Add usage reset time to status line metadata for customization · Issue #7185 · anthropics/claude-code","url":"https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/7185"},{"title":"GitHub - chongdashu/cc-statusline: Transform your Claude Code experience with a beautiful, informative statusline","url":"https://github.com/chongdashu/cc-statusline"}]

Here are the search results for Claude Code status line configuration and terminal customization:

## Official Documentation

Claude Code offers custom status line functionality that displays at the bottom of the Claude Code interface, similar to how terminal prompts (PS1) work in shells like Oh-my-zsh.

### Basic Setup
Run `/statusline` to ask Claude Code to help you set up a custom status line. By default, it will try to reproduce your terminal's prompt, but you can provide additional instructions. You can also directly add a statusLine command to your `.claude/settings.json` file with a configuration like:
```json
{
  "statusLine": {
    "type": "command",
    "command": "~/.claude/statusline.sh",
    "padding": 0
  }
}
```(no content)Claude Code passes contextual information about the current session (model, directories, etc.) as JSON to your script via stdin.

## Popular Third-Party Tools

### ccstatusline
CC Statusline (by @sirmalloc) has 2.3k stars on GitHub and was updated on Dec 12, 2025.(no content)It features multi-line support, interactive TUI configuration using React/Ink, global formatting options, cross-platform support with Bun and Node.js, and zero config defaults.

Installation: `npx ccstatusline@latest` or `bunx ccstatusline@latest`

### claude-code-statusline (rz1989s)
This tool offers a single config location at `~/.claude/statusline/Config.toml`, live reload with `--watch-config`, built-in themes (Classic, Garden, Catppuccin Mocha), real-time cost tracking, MCP server monitoring, and block reset timer tracking.

### cc-statusline (chongdashu)
Offers a Bash-first approach with native shell execution for maximum speed, Claude Code optimized color forcing, and zero dependencies with self-contained scripts.

## Key Use Cases

A popular use case is monitoring the percentage of context window used in a session. As one guide notes, monitoring context is important because "the more you put in the context window, the worse the AI is going to be" and having this visible helps users know when to stop around 60%.(no content)Claude Code has a 200K token context window and automatically runs `/compact` when you hit around 80% usage. Without a custom statusline, you won't know you're approaching the limit until it's too late.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Here are the search results for Claude Code subagents configuration and specialized agents on GitHub:

## Official Documentation

Custom subagents in Claude Code are specialized AI assistants that can be invoked to handle specific types of tasks. They enable more efficient problem-solving by providing task-specific configurations with customized system prompts, tools and a separate context window.

### Subagent Configuration Structure
Each subagent is defined in a Markdown file with a YAML frontmatter containing name, description, tools (optional), model (optional), permissionMode, and skills. The body contains the system prompt that should clearly define the subagent's role, capabilities, and approach to solving problems.(no content)The model field allows you to control which AI model the subagent uses: Model alias (sonnet, opus, or haiku), 'inherit' to use the same model as the main conversation, or if omitted, uses the default model (sonnet).

## Popular GitHub Collections

Claude Code automatically detects and loads the subagents.(no content)Each subagent's tools field specifies Claude Code built-in tools optimized for their role: Read-only agents get Read, Grep, Glob; Research agents get WebFetch and WebSearch; Code writers get Read, Write, Edit, Bash.

### 2. **wshobson/agents**
A comprehensive production-ready system combining 91 specialized AI agents, 15 multi-agent workflow orchestrators, 47 agent skills, and 45 development tools organized into 65 focused, single-purpose plugins for Claude Code.

Here are the search results for Claude Code tips, tricks, and optimization workflows from 2025:

## Key Tips and Tricks

### Permission Management
Every time you open Claude Code, you can run `claude --dangerously-skip-permissions` to skip all permission prompts. It's not as dangerous as it sounds — think of it as Cursor's old "yolo mode."(no content)You can speed up your workflow by pre-approving safe and frequent operations. Use the `claude allow [command]` command to add trusted tools and commands to an allowlist when constantly interrupted by [y/n] prompts for common commands like git commit or npm install.

### CLAUDE.md Configuration
"CLAUDE.md is a special file that Claude automatically pulls into context when starting a conversation." It's ideal for documenting common bash commands, core files, code style guidelines, testing instructions, and repository etiquette.(no content)Optimizing the system prompt yielded 5%+ gains in general coding performance. Repository-specific optimization can yield "+11% better code" by training Claude Code to internalize a repo's patterns, norms, and conventions.

### Custom Slash Commands
For repeated workflows—debugging loops, log analysis, etc.—store prompt templates in Markdown files within the `.claude/commands` folder. These become available through the slash commands menu when you type `/`. You can check these commands into git to make them available for the rest of your team.(no content)Custom slash commands let you encode repeatable processes and workflows that are specific to your team or project. You first setup a folder to store the custom commands as markdown files.

### Modes and Thinking Depth
Plan Mode engages Claude's extended thinking capabilities to create comprehensive strategies. Use it when starting a new feature, tackling complex challenges, or refactoring code. You can control the depth of analysis with specific phrases like "think", "think hard", and "ultrathink" for increasing depth.

### Keyboard Shortcuts & UI Tips
Stopping Claude isn't Control+C (that just exits entirely). Use Escape to actually stop Claude. Escape twice shows a list of all previous messages you can jump back to.(no content)Pasting images from clipboard doesn't work with Command+V. Use Control+V instead.(no content)Shift+Enter doesn't work by default for new lines. Just tell Claude to set up your terminal with `/terminal-setup` and it'll fix it.

### Advanced Workflows
Ask Claude to read relevant files first without writing code yet. This is the part where you should consider strong use of subagents, especially for complex problems. Telling Claude to use subagents to verify details early in a conversation tends to preserve context availability without much downside.(no content)Run multiple Claude sessions simultaneously to handle complex tasks collaboratively or in parallel. Use git worktree to check out multiple branches into different directories and run a separate Claude session in each to work on different features in parallel.(no content)You can customize the status line at the bottom of Claude Code to show useful info like the model, current directory, git branch, uncommitted file count, sync status with origin, and a visual progress bar for token usage.

### Context Management
Start new conversations regularly. Fresh sessions using `/clear` reduce prompt-drift and prevent context contamination from earlier tasks.(no content)Clear, structured prompting cuts errors and slashes back-and-forths by specifying exact tasks, files, and expected outputs using XML-like tags for precision. Context management commands like `/init` and `/clear` keep Claude focused across sessions.

### GitHub Integration
One of the cooler slash commands is `/install-github-app`. After you run it, Claude will automatically review your PRs. This is useful because as you use more AI tools, your PR volume increases.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.
